title: Donald Trump

infobox.value: Donald John Trump  (1946-06-14) June 14, 1946 (age 79)  [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), New York City, U.S.

* [Reform](/wiki/Reform_Party_of_the_United_States_of_America "Reform Party of the United States of America") (1999–2001) * [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") (2001–2009) * [Independent](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") (2011–2012)

**Donald John Trump** (born June 14, 1946) is an American politician, media personality, and businessman who is the 47th [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"). A member of the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), he served as the 45th president from 2017 to 2021.

Born into a wealthy New York City family, Trump graduated from the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") in 1968 with a [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") in economics. He became the president of his family's real estate business in 1971, renamed it [the Trump Organization](/wiki/The_Trump_Organization "The Trump Organization"), and began acquiring and building skyscrapers, hotels, casinos, and golf courses. He launched side ventures, many licensing the Trump name, and filed for six business bankruptcies in the 1990s and 2000s. From 2004 to 2015, he hosted the reality television show *[The Apprentice](/wiki/The_Apprentice_(American_TV_series) "The Apprentice (American TV series)")*, bolstering his fame as a billionaire. Presenting himself as a political outsider, Trump won the [2016 presidential election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election") against [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") nominee [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton").

During [his first presidency](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "First presidency of Donald Trump"), Trump imposed [a travel ban](/wiki/Executive_Order_13769 "Executive Order 13769") on seven Muslim-majority countries, expanded the [Mexico–United States border wall](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_wall "Mexico–United States border wall"), and enforced [a family separation policy](/wiki/Trump_administration_family_separation_policy "Trump administration family separation policy") on the border. He rolled back environmental and business regulations, signed the [Tax Cuts and Jobs Act](/wiki/Tax_Cuts_and_Jobs_Act "Tax Cuts and Jobs Act"), and appointed three Supreme Court justices. He withdrew the U.S. from agreements on climate, trade, and Iran's nuclear program, and started [a trade war with China](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war "China–United States trade war"). In response to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States") in 2020, he downplayed its severity, contradicted health officials, and signed the [CARES Act](/wiki/CARES_Act "CARES Act"). After losing the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") to [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden"), Trump [attempted to overturn the result](/wiki/Attempts_to_overturn_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election "Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election"), culminating in the [January 6 Capitol attack](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack") in 2021. He was [impeached in 2019](/wiki/First_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "First impeachment of Donald Trump") for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress, and [in 2021](/wiki/Second_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "Second impeachment of Donald Trump") for incitement of insurrection; the Senate acquitted him both times.

In 2023, Trump was found liable in civil cases [for sexual abuse and defamation](/wiki/E._Jean_Carroll_v._Donald_J._Trump "E. Jean Carroll v. Donald J. Trump") and [for business fraud](/wiki/New_York_business_fraud_lawsuit_against_the_Trump_Organization "New York business fraud lawsuit against the Trump Organization"). He was found guilty in 34 counts [of falsifying business records](/wiki/Prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_in_New_York "Prosecution of Donald Trump in New York") in 2024, making him the first U.S. president convicted of a felony. After winning the [2024 presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election") against Vice President [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris"), he was sentenced to a [discharge](/wiki/Discharge_(sentence) "Discharge (sentence)"), and two [federal felony indictments against him](/wiki/Indictments_against_Donald_Trump "Indictments against Donald Trump") for [retention of classified documents](/wiki/Federal_prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_(classified_documents_case) "Federal prosecution of Donald Trump (classified documents case)") and [obstruction of the 2020 election](/wiki/Federal_prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_(election_obstruction_case) "Federal prosecution of Donald Trump (election obstruction case)") were dismissed without prejudice.

Trump began [his second presidency](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump") by initiating [mass layoffs of federal workers](/wiki/2025_United_States_federal_mass_layoffs "2025 United States federal mass layoffs"). He [imposed tariffs](/wiki/Tariffs_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Tariffs in the second Trump administration") on nearly all countries at the highest level since the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and signed the [One Big Beautiful Bill Act](/wiki/One_Big_Beautiful_Bill_Act "One Big Beautiful Bill Act"). His administration's actions—including its [targeting of political opponents](/wiki/Targeting_of_political_opponents_and_civil_society_under_the_second_Trump_administration "Targeting of political opponents and civil society under the second Trump administration") and civil society, [persecution of transgender people](/wiki/Persecution_of_transgender_people_under_the_second_Trump_administration "Persecution of transgender people under the second Trump administration"), mass [deportation of immigrants](/wiki/Deportation_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Deportation in the second Trump administration"), and extensive use of executive orders—have drawn over 550 [lawsuits challenging their legality](/wiki/Legal_affairs_of_the_second_Trump_presidency "Legal affairs of the second Trump presidency"). In Latin America, he pursued a legally contested campaign to [attack alleged drug traffickers](/wiki/United_States_strikes_on_alleged_drug_traffickers_during_Operation_Southern_Spear "United States strikes on alleged drug traffickers during Operation Southern Spear"), and ordered [a military raid into Venezuela](/wiki/2026_United_States_intervention_in_Venezuela "2026 United States intervention in Venezuela") to capture the country's president. In February 2026, Trump authorized joint U.S.–Israeli strikes on Iran that resulted in the [2026 Iran war](/wiki/2026_Iran_war "2026 Iran war").

Since 2015, Trump's leadership style and political agenda—often referred to as [Trumpism](/wiki/Trumpism "Trumpism")—have reshaped the Republican Party's identity. Many of his comments and actions have been [characterized as racist](/wiki/Racial_views_of_Donald_Trump "Racial views of Donald Trump") or misogynistic. He has [made many false or misleading statements](/wiki/False_or_misleading_statements_by_Donald_Trump "False or misleading statements by Donald Trump") during his campaigns and presidency, to a degree unprecedented in American politics, and [promotes conspiracy theories](/wiki/List_of_conspiracy_theories_promoted_by_Donald_Trump "List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump"). Trump's actions have been described by researchers as authoritarian and contributing to [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding_in_the_United_States "Democratic backsliding in the United States"). After his first term, scholars and historians [ranked him](#Scholarly_rankings) as one of the worst presidents in American history.

## Early life and education

Main article: [Early life and education of Donald Trump](/wiki/Early_life_and_education_of_Donald_Trump "Early life and education of Donald Trump")

[![A black-and-white photograph of Trump as a teenager, smiling, wearing a dark pseudo-military uniform with three ribbons and a white shoulder strap](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Donald_Trump_NYMA.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_NYMA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_NYMA.jpg)

1964 yearbook photo

Donald John Trump was born on June 14, 1946, at [Jamaica Hospital](/wiki/Jamaica_Hospital "Jamaica Hospital") in the New York City borough of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), the fourth child of [Fred Trump](/wiki/Fred_Trump "Fred Trump") and [Mary Anne MacLeod Trump](/wiki/Mary_Anne_MacLeod_Trump "Mary Anne MacLeod Trump").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher201730,_37-1) He is of German and Scottish descent.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher2017v-2) He grew up with his older siblings, [Maryanne](/wiki/Maryanne_Trump_Barry "Maryanne Trump Barry"), [Fred Jr.](/wiki/Fred_Trump_Jr. "Fred Trump Jr."), and Elizabeth, and his younger brother, [Robert](/wiki/Robert_Trump "Robert Trump"), in a 23-room mansion in the [Jamaica Estates](/wiki/Jamaica_Estates "Jamaica Estates") neighborhood of Queens.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Fred Trump paid his children each about $20,000 a year, equivalent to $265,000 a year in 2024. Trump was a millionaire in inflation-adjusted dollars by age eight.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuettnerCraig202430–31-4)

Trump attended the private [Kew-Forest School](/wiki/Kew-Forest_School "Kew-Forest School") through seventh grade. His father enrolled him in the [New York Military Academy](/wiki/New_York_Military_Academy "New York Military Academy"), a private boarding school, from eighth to twelfth grade.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher201733,_38,_45-5) The academy pushed students into sports[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuettnerCraig202466-6) and taught the imperative of winning.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher201742-7)

Trump considered a show business career but instead, to be closer to home, enrolled at [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University") in 1964.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher201745–47-8) He participated in the [Reserve Officers' Training Corps](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps") during his first year, attending classes in a military uniform every Wednesday, but dropped it in his second year.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuettnerCraig202499-9) In his junior year, he transferred to the [Wharton School](/wiki/Wharton_School "Wharton School") of the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania"), most often commuting to his father's office on weekends, and graduated in May 1968 with a [Bachelor of Science](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science") in economics.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher201747–50-10) Contrary to his statements that he was top of his class with the highest grades possible, Wharton's published academic honors and dean's list do not include his name.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher201747-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) By the time he went to Wharton he was eyeing a career in real estate.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrett201675-13) He was exempted from the draft during the Vietnam War due to a claim of [bone spurs](/wiki/Bone_spurs "Bone spurs") in his heels.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher201748-14)

## Business career

Main article: [Business career of Donald Trump](/wiki/Business_career_of_Donald_Trump "Business career of Donald Trump")

Further information: [Business projects of Donald Trump in Russia](/wiki/Business_projects_of_Donald_Trump_in_Russia "Business projects of Donald Trump in Russia"), [Tax returns of Donald Trump](/wiki/Tax_returns_of_Donald_Trump "Tax returns of Donald Trump"), and [The Trump Organization](/wiki/The_Trump_Organization "The Trump Organization")

### Real estate

See also: [The Trump Organization § Real Estate](/wiki/The_Trump_Organization#Real_Estate "The Trump Organization")

Starting in 1968, Trump was employed at Trump Management, his father's real estate company,[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher201750–51-15) which managed the middle-class apartment complexes Fred had built in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"), and [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher201752–53-16) His main tasks were collecting rent and making repairs[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrett201676-17) for about five years.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Brien2005a51-18) Trump asked his father to expand to [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") where prices were higher, but his father was content in the outer boroughs.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Brien2005a51-18) In 1971, he moved to Manhattan, where he planned to move the business[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrett201679-19) and commuted to his father's office.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher201759-20) That year, his father made himself chairman and Trump president.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrett201675-13) Trump began using the Trump Organization as an umbrella for the corporate names of his father's businesses.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlair2015250-21)

[Roy Cohn](/wiki/Roy_Cohn "Roy Cohn"), Trump's most important early influence after his father,[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrett201681-22) was his [fixer](/wiki/Fixer_(person) "Fixer (person)"), lawyer, and mentor[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrett2016126-23) for 13 years in the 1970s and 1980s.[[24]](#cite_note-Mahler2016Cohn-24) Cohn taught Trump to think that life is transactional.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaberman202233-25) In 1973, Cohn helped Trump countersue the U.S. government for $100 million (equivalent to $725 million in 2025[[26]](#cite_note-inflation-US-26)) over its charges that Trump's properties had discriminated against Black applicants and tenants. The case was settled in a consent decree agreeing to desegregate, which the Trumps ended up in court for violating four years later.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrett201682–84-27) Helping Trump projects,[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrett2016190–191-28) Cohn was a [consigliere](/wiki/Consigliere "Consigliere") whose Mafia connections controlled construction unions.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnston201645–46-29) In 1979, Cohn introduced political consultant [Roger Stone](/wiki/Roger_Stone "Roger Stone") to Trump, who enlisted Stone's services to deal with the federal government.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Trump showed a propensity for litigation, no matter the outcome and cost; even when he lost, he described the case as a win.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuettnerCraig2024126-31) By 2018, Trump had been involved in more than 4,000 lawsuits,[[32]](#cite_note-usat-lawsuits-32) liens, and other filings, often filed for nonpayment against him by employees, contractors, real estate brokers, and his own attorneys.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Between 1991 and 2009, Trump filed for [Chapter 11](/wiki/Chapter_11 "Chapter 11") bankruptcy protection for six of his businesses: the [Plaza Hotel](/wiki/Plaza_Hotel "Plaza Hotel") in Manhattan, the casinos in [Atlantic City, New Jersey](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey"), and the [Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts](/wiki/Trump_Hotels_%26_Casino_Resorts "Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts") company.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-TW-35)

In 1992 and 1994, Trump, working with several relatives, formed a shell company for paying the vendors providing services and supplies for Trump's rental units, then billing those services and supplies to Trump Management with significant markups; the increased costs were used to get state approval for increasing the rents of his rent-stabilized units. Besides inflating rents, the schemes served to transfer assets from Fred Trump to his children and nephew and lower their tax burden.[[36]](#cite_note-Tax_Schemes-36)

#### Manhattan and Chicago developments

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Donald_Trump_with_model_of_Television_City.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_with_model_of_Television_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_with_model_of_Television_City.jpg)

In 1985 with a model of one of his aborted Manhattan development projects[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Trump gained public attention in 1978 with the launch of his family's first Manhattan venture: the renovation of the derelict [Commodore Hotel](/wiki/Hyatt_Grand_Central_New_York#Commodore_Hotel "Hyatt Grand Central New York"), adjacent to Grand Central Terminal.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The financing was facilitated by a $400 million city property tax abatement arranged for him by his father who also, jointly with [Hyatt](/wiki/Hyatt "Hyatt"), guaranteed a $70 million bank construction loan.[[39]](#cite_note-Rich_NYMag-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) The hotel reopened in 1980 as the [Grand Hyatt Hotel](/wiki/Hyatt_Grand_Central_New_York "Hyatt Grand Central New York"),[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher2017[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidx2jUDQAAQBAJpgPA84_84]-41) and that same year, he obtained rights to develop [Trump Tower](/wiki/Trump_Tower "Trump Tower"), a mixed-use skyscraper in Midtown Manhattan.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The building houses the headquarters of the Trump Corporation and Trump's [PAC](/wiki/Political_action_committee "Political action committee") and was his primary residence until 2019.[[43]](#cite_note-moved-43) In 1988, Trump acquired the Plaza Hotel with a loan from a consortium of 16 banks.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The hotel filed for bankruptcy protection in 1992, and a reorganization plan was approved a month later, with the banks taking control of the property.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In 1995, Trump defaulted on over $3 billion of bank loans, and the lenders seized the Plaza Hotel along with most of his other properties in a "vast and humiliating restructuring" that allowed him to avoid personal bankruptcy.[[46]](#cite_note-plaza-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) Trump's last major construction project was the 92-story mixed-use [Trump International Hotel and Tower](/wiki/Trump_International_Hotel_and_Tower_(Chicago) "Trump International Hotel and Tower (Chicago)") in Chicago, which opened in 2008. In 2024, [*The New York Times* and ProPublica reported](/wiki/Trump_International_Hotel_and_Tower_(Chicago)#Tax_deductions "Trump International Hotel and Tower (Chicago)") that the Internal Revenue Service was investigating whether he had twice written off losses incurred through construction cost overruns and lagging sales of residential units in the building he had declared to be worthless on his 2008 tax return.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### Atlantic City casinos

[![The entrance of the Trump Taj Mahal, a casino in Atlantic City. It has motifs evocative of the Taj Mahal in India.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Trump_Taj_Mahal%2C_2007.jpg/250px-Trump_Taj_Mahal%2C_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trump_Taj_Mahal,_2007.jpg)

Entrance of the Trump Taj Mahal (now the [Hard Rock Hotel & Casino](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Hotel_%26_Casino_Atlantic_City "Hard Rock Hotel & Casino Atlantic City")) in [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City "Atlantic City")

In 1984, Trump opened [Harrah's at Trump Plaza](/wiki/Harrah%27s_at_Trump_Plaza "Harrah's at Trump Plaza"), a hotel and casino, with financing and management help from the [Holiday Corporation](/wiki/Holiday_Corporation "Holiday Corporation").[[49]](#cite_note-fall-49) It was unprofitable, and he paid Holiday $70 million in May 1986 to take sole control.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher2017[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidx2jUDQAAQBAJpgPA128_128]-50) In 1985, he bought the unopened Atlantic City Hilton Hotel and renamed it [Trump's Castle](/wiki/Trump%27s_Castle "Trump's Castle").[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlair2015351-52) Both casinos filed for [Chapter 11 bankruptcy](/wiki/Chapter_11_bankruptcy "Chapter 11 bankruptcy") protection in 1992.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Trump bought a third Atlantic City venue in 1988, the [Trump Taj Mahal](/wiki/Trump_Taj_Mahal "Trump Taj Mahal"). It was financed with $675 million in [junk bonds](/wiki/Junk_bonds "Junk bonds") and completed for $1.1 billion, opening in April 1990.[[49]](#cite_note-fall-49) He filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in 1991. Under the provisions of the restructuring agreement, Trump gave up half his initial stake and personally guaranteed future performance.[[54]](#cite_note-54) To reduce his $900 million of personal debt, he sold the [Trump Shuttle](/wiki/Trump_Shuttle "Trump Shuttle") airline; his megayacht, the *[Trump Princess](/wiki/Trump_Princess "Trump Princess")*, which had been leased to his casinos and kept docked; and other businesses.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In 1995, Trump founded Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR), which assumed ownership of the Trump Plaza.[[56]](#cite_note-56) THCR purchased the Taj Mahal and the Trump Castle in 1996 and went bankrupt in 2004 and 2009, leaving him with 10 percent ownership.[[49]](#cite_note-fall-49) He remained chairman until 2009.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

#### Golf clubs

In 1985, Trump acquired the [Mar-a-Lago](/wiki/Mar-a-Lago "Mar-a-Lago") estate in Palm Beach, Florida.[[58]](#cite_note-58) In 1995, he converted the estate into a private club with an initiation fee and annual dues. Trump continued to use a wing of the house as a private residence.[[59]](#cite_note-59) He declared the club his primary residence in 2019.[[43]](#cite_note-moved-43) Trump began [building and buying golf courses](/wiki/Donald_Trump_and_golf "Donald Trump and golf") in 1999, owning 17 golf courses globally by 2016.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Licensing the Trump name

See also: [List of things named after Donald Trump](/wiki/List_of_things_named_after_Donald_Trump "List of things named after Donald Trump")

[The Trump Organization](/wiki/The_Trump_Organization "The Trump Organization") often licensed the Trump name for consumer products and services, including foodstuffs, apparel, learning courses, and home furnishings.[[61]](#cite_note-neckties-61) Over 50 licensing or management deals involved his name, generating at least $59 million for his companies.[[62]](#cite_note-62) By 2018, only two consumer goods companies continued to license Trump's name.[[61]](#cite_note-neckties-61) During the 2000s, he licensed his name to real estate developments. Forty of the projects he announced were not completed.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuettnerCraig2024410-63)

### Side ventures

See also: [Donald Trump and American football](/wiki/Donald_Trump_and_American_football "Donald Trump and American football")

[![Trump, Doug Flutie, and New Jersey Generals head coach Walt Michaels standing behind a lectern with big, round New Jersey Generals sign, with members of the press seated in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Donald_Trump_and_Doug_Flutie_at_a_press_conference_in_the_Trump_Tower.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_and_Doug_Flutie_at_a_press_conference_in_the_Trump_Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_and_Doug_Flutie_at_a_press_conference_in_the_Trump_Tower.jpg)

1985 New Jersey Generals press conference in [Trump Tower](/wiki/Trump_Tower "Trump Tower")

In 1970, Trump invested $70,000 of his father's wealth to receive billing as coproducer of a Broadway comedy—and lost the money.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuettnerCraig2024109-64) After making low-ball bids for the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") and the [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians "Cleveland Indians") baseball teams, in 1983 for about $6 million, he purchased the [New Jersey Generals](/wiki/New_Jersey_Generals "New Jersey Generals"), a team in the [United States Football League](/wiki/United_States_Football_League "United States Football League").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher2017175-65) The league folded after the 1985 season, largely due to his attempt to move to a fall schedule (when it would have competed with the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") for audience) and his attempt to force a merger with the NFL by bringing an antitrust suit.[[66]](#cite_note-66) In 1989 and 1990, he lent his name to the [Tour de Trump](/wiki/Tour_de_Trump "Tour de Trump") cycling stage race, an attempt to create an American equivalent of European races such as the [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France") or the [Giro d'Italia](/wiki/Giro_d%27Italia "Giro d'Italia").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

From 1986 to 1988, he purchased significant blocks of shares in various public companies while suggesting that he intended to take over the company and then sold his shares for a profit,[[68]](#cite_note-Buettner-190508-68) leading some observers to think he was engaged in [greenmail](/wiki/Greenmail "Greenmail").[[69]](#cite_note-69) *The New York Times* found that he initially made millions of dollars in such stock transactions, but "lost most, if not all, of those gains after investors stopped taking his takeover talk seriously".[[68]](#cite_note-Buettner-190508-68)

[![A red star with a bronze outline and "Donald Trump" and a TV icon written on it in bronze, embedded in a black terrazzo sidewalk](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Donald_Trump_star_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame.JPG/250px-Donald_Trump_star_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame.JPG)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_star_Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame.JPG)

Trump's star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame")

In 1988, Trump purchased the [Eastern Air Lines Shuttle](/wiki/Eastern_Air_Lines_Shuttle "Eastern Air Lines Shuttle"), financing the purchase with $380 million (equivalent to $1.03 billion in 2025[[26]](#cite_note-inflation-US-26)) in loans from a syndicate of 22 banks. He renamed the airline [Trump Shuttle](/wiki/Trump_Shuttle "Trump Shuttle") and operated it until 1992.[[70]](#cite_note-TA-70) He defaulted on his loans in 1991, and ownership passed to the banks.[[71]](#cite_note-71) In 1996, he purchased the [Miss Universe](/wiki/Miss_Universe "Miss Universe") pageants, including [Miss USA](/wiki/Miss_USA "Miss USA") and [Miss Teen USA](/wiki/Miss_Teen_USA "Miss Teen USA").[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaberman2022129–130-72) Due to disagreements with [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") about scheduling, he took both pageants to [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") in 2002.[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) In 2007, he received a star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") for his work as producer of Miss Universe.[[75]](#cite_note-75) NBC and Univision dropped the pageants in June 2015 in reaction to his comments about Mexican immigrants.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

#### Trump University

In 2005, Trump cofounded [Trump University](/wiki/Trump_University "Trump University"), a company that sold real estate seminars for up to $35,000. After New York State authorities notified the company that its use of "university" violated state law because it was not an academic institution, its name was changed to the Trump Entrepreneur Initiative in 2010.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTED'Antonio2015281–282-77) In 2013, the State of New York filed a $40 million civil suit against Trump University, alleging that the company made false statements and defrauded consumers. Additionally, two class actions were filed in federal court against Trump and his companies. Internal documents revealed that employees were instructed to use a hard-sell approach, and former employees testified that Trump University had defrauded or lied to its students.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTED'Antonio2015282–283-78) Shortly after he won the 2016 presidential election, he agreed to pay a total of $25 million to settle the three cases.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

### Foundation

Main article: [Donald J. Trump Foundation](/wiki/Donald_J._Trump_Foundation "Donald J. Trump Foundation")

The Donald J. Trump Foundation was a [private foundation](/wiki/Private_foundation_(United_States) "Private foundation (United States)") established in 1988.[[80]](#cite_note-80) From 1987 to 2006, Trump gave his foundation $5.4 million, which had been spent by the end of 2006. After donating a total of $65,000 in 2007–2008, he stopped donating any personal funds to the charity,[[81]](#cite_note-retool-81) which received millions from other donors, including $5 million from [Vince McMahon](/wiki/Vince_McMahon "Vince McMahon").[[82]](#cite_note-82) The foundation gave to health and sports-related charities, conservative groups,[[83]](#cite_note-83) and charities that held events at Trump properties.[[81]](#cite_note-retool-81) In 2016, *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* reported that the charity had committed several potential legal and ethical violations, including self-dealing and [tax evasion](/wiki/Tax_evasion "Tax evasion").[[84]](#cite_note-84) Also in 2016, the New York attorney general stated the foundation had violated state law by soliciting donations without submitting to required annual external audits and ordered it to cease its fundraising activities in New York immediately.[[85]](#cite_note-85) Trump's team announced in December 2016 that the foundation would be dissolved.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

In June 2018, the New York attorney general's office filed a civil suit against the foundation, Trump, and his adult children, seeking $2.8 million in restitution and additional penalties.[[87]](#cite_note-87) In December 2018, the foundation ceased operation and disbursed its assets to other charities.[[88]](#cite_note-88) In November 2019, a New York state judge ordered Trump to pay $2 million to a group of charities for misusing the foundation's funds, in part to finance his presidential campaign.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

### Legal affairs and bankruptcies

Main article: [Personal and business legal affairs of Donald Trump](/wiki/Personal_and_business_legal_affairs_of_Donald_Trump "Personal and business legal affairs of Donald Trump")

According to a review of state and federal court files conducted by *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")* in 2018, Trump and his businesses had been involved in more than 4,000 state and federal legal actions.[[32]](#cite_note-usat-lawsuits-32) While Trump has not filed for [personal bankruptcy](/wiki/Personal_bankruptcy "Personal bankruptcy"), his over-leveraged hotel and casino businesses in Atlantic City and New York filed for [Chapter 11 bankruptcy](/wiki/Chapter_11_bankruptcy "Chapter 11 bankruptcy") protection six times between 1991 and 2009.[[35]](#cite_note-TW-35) They continued to operate while the banks restructured debt and reduced his shares in the properties.[[35]](#cite_note-TW-35) During the 1980s, more than 70 banks had lent Trump $4 billion.[[90]](#cite_note-90) After his corporate bankruptcies of the early 1990s, most major banks, with the exception of [Deutsche Bank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank "Deutsche Bank"), declined to lend to him.[[91]](#cite_note-91) After the [January 6 Capitol attack](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack"), Deutsche Bank also decided not to do business with him or his affiliated company in the future.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

## Media career

Main article: [Media career of Donald Trump](/wiki/Media_career_of_Donald_Trump "Media career of Donald Trump")

Trump has [published 19 books under his name](/wiki/Bibliography_of_Donald_Trump "Bibliography of Donald Trump"), most written or cowritten by [ghostwriters](/wiki/Ghostwriters "Ghostwriters").[[93]](#cite_note-93) His first book, *[The Art of the Deal](/wiki/The_Art_of_the_Deal "The Art of the Deal")* (1987), was a [*New York Times* Best Seller](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Best_Seller_list "The New York Times Best Seller list"), and was credited by *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")* with making Trump famous as an "emblem of the successful tycoon".[[94]](#cite_note-JM-94) The book was ghostwritten by [Tony Schwartz](/wiki/Tony_Schwartz_(author) "Tony Schwartz (author)"), who is credited as a coauthor. Trump had cameos in many films and television shows from 1985 to 2001.[[95]](#cite_note-95) Trump acquired his style of politics from [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling"). From the late 1980s, he sporadically played himself as a super-rich boss at events staged by professional wrestling promotion [WWE](/wiki/WWE "WWE"), including [WrestleMania 23](/wiki/WrestleMania_23 "WrestleMania 23") in 2007.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaufmanGanesh202469–70-96)[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'Brien2020-97)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Starting in the 1990s, Trump appeared 24 times as a guest on the nationally syndicated *[Howard Stern Show](/wiki/Howard_Stern_Show "Howard Stern Show")*.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher2017[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidx2jUDQAAQBAJpgPA166_166]-98) He had his own short-form talk radio program, *[Trumped!](/wiki/Trumped! "Trumped!")*, from 2004 to 2008.[[99]](#cite_note-99) From 2011 until 2015, he was a guest commentator on *[Fox & Friends](/wiki/Fox_%26_Friends "Fox & Friends")*.[[100]](#cite_note-100) In 2021, Trump, who had been a member since 1989, resigned from [SAG-AFTRA](/wiki/SAG-AFTRA "SAG-AFTRA") to avoid a disciplinary hearing regarding the January 6 attack; two days later, the union permanently barred him.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Producer [Mark Burnett](/wiki/Mark_Burnett "Mark Burnett") made Trump a television star[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuettnerCraig20247-102) when he created the reality show [*The Apprentice*](/wiki/The_Apprentice_(American_TV_series) "The Apprentice (American TV series)"), which Trump hosted from 2004 to 2015 (including variant *[The Celebrity Apprentice](/wiki/The_Celebrity_Apprentice "The Celebrity Apprentice")*). On the shows, he was a superrich chief executive who eliminated contestants with the [catchphrase](/wiki/Catchphrase "Catchphrase") "you're fired". *The New York Times* called his portrayal "a highly flattering, highly fictionalized version" of himself.[[103]](#cite_note-show-103) The shows remade Trump's image for millions of viewers nationwide.[[103]](#cite_note-show-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) With the related licensing agreements, they earned him more than $400 million.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

## Early political aspirations

Further information: [Political career of Donald Trump](/wiki/Political_career_of_Donald_Trump "Political career of Donald Trump")

Trump registered as a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") in Queens in 1969 and in Manhattan in 1987;[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarrett201675-13)[[106]](#cite_note-106) a member of the [Independence Party](/wiki/Independence_Party_of_New_York "Independence Party of New York"), the New York state affiliate of the [Reform Party](/wiki/Reform_Party_of_the_United_States_of_America "Reform Party of the United States of America"), in 1999; a [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") in 2001; a Republican in 2009; [unaffiliated](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") in 2011; and a Republican in 2012.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

[![Trump, leaning heavily onto a lectern, with his mouth open mid-speech and a woman clapping next to him](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Donald_Trump_speaking_at_CPAC_2011_by_Mark_Taylor.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_speaking_at_CPAC_2011_by_Mark_Taylor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_speaking_at_CPAC_2011_by_Mark_Taylor.jpg)

Speaking at [CPAC](/wiki/Conservative_Political_Action_Conference "Conservative Political Action Conference"), February 2011

In 1987, Trump placed full-page advertisements in major newspapers,[[108]](#cite_note-hint-108) expressing his views on foreign policy and how to eliminate the federal budget deficit.[[109]](#cite_note-109) In 1988, he approached [Lee Atwater](/wiki/Lee_Atwater "Lee Atwater"), asking to be put into consideration to be Republican nominee [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush")'s running mate. Bush found the request "strange and unbelievable".[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeacham2016326-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111) Trump [was a candidate](/wiki/Donald_Trump_2000_presidential_campaign "Donald Trump 2000 presidential campaign") in the [2000 Reform Party presidential primaries](/wiki/2000_Reform_Party_presidential_primaries "2000 Reform Party presidential primaries") for three months before he withdrew in February 2000.[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114) In 2011, Trump considered challenging President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in [the 2012 election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election"). He spoke at the [Conservative Political Action Conference](/wiki/Conservative_Political_Action_Conference "Conservative Political Action Conference") in February and gave speeches in states with early primaries.[[115]](#cite_note-McA-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116) In May 2011, he announced that he would not run.[[115]](#cite_note-McA-115)

## 2016 presidential election

Main article: [2016 United States presidential election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election")

Further information: [Donald Trump 2016 presidential campaign](/wiki/Donald_Trump_2016_presidential_campaign "Donald Trump 2016 presidential campaign"), [2016 Republican Party presidential primaries](/wiki/2016_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "2016 Republican Party presidential primaries"), and [First presidential transition of Donald Trump](/wiki/First_presidential_transition_of_Donald_Trump "First presidential transition of Donald Trump")

Trump announced his candidacy for the 2016 election in June 2015.[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118) He campaigned as a rich, successful businessman and an outsider without political experience,[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121) and claimed [media bias](/wiki/Media_bias "Media bias") against him.[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123) Trump's campaign statements were often opaque and suggestive;[[124]](#cite_note-124) a record number were false.[[125]](#cite_note-whoppers-125)[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-127) He became the Republican front-runner in March 2016[[128]](#cite_note-128) and was declared the presumptive Republican nominee in May.[[129]](#cite_note-129) This and later campaigns featured the slogan "[Make America Great Again](/wiki/Make_America_Great_Again "Make America Great Again")".

[![Trump speaking in front of an American flag behind a lectern, wearing a black business suit and a bright red "MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN" baseball cap. The lectern sports a blue "TRUMP" sign.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Donald_Trump_by_Gage_Skidmore_5.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_by_Gage_Skidmore_5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_by_Gage_Skidmore_5.jpg)

Campaigning in Arizona, March 2016

Trump described [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") as "obsolete"[[130]](#cite_note-130)[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards2018"On_the_campaign_trail,_Trump_repeatedly_called_North_Atlantic_Treaty_Organization_(NATO)_'obsolete'"-131) and espoused views described by *The Washington Post* as [noninterventionist](/wiki/Noninterventionist "Noninterventionist") and [protectionist](/wiki/Protectionist "Protectionist").[[132]](#cite_note-132) His campaign platform emphasized renegotiating [U.S.–China relations](/wiki/U.S.%E2%80%93China_relations "U.S.–China relations") and [free trade agreements](/wiki/Free_trade_agreements "Free trade agreements") such as [NAFTA](/wiki/NAFTA "NAFTA") and strongly enforcing immigration laws. Other campaign positions included pursuing [energy independence](/wiki/Energy_independence "Energy independence") while opposing climate change regulations, modernizing [services for veterans](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Veterans_Affairs#Veterans_Benefits_Administration "United States Department of Veterans Affairs"), repealing and replacing the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act"), abolishing [Common Core](/wiki/Common_Core "Common Core") education standards, [investing in infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure-based_development "Infrastructure-based development"), simplifying the [tax code](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Code "Internal Revenue Code") while reducing taxes, and imposing [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff") on imports by companies that offshore jobs. He advocated increasing military spending and extreme vetting or banning of immigrants from Muslim-majority countries.[[133]](#cite_note-133) He promised to build a [wall on the Mexico–U.S. border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_wall "Mexico–United States border wall") and vowed that Mexico would pay for it.[[134]](#cite_note-134) He pledged to deport millions of [illegal immigrants residing in the U.S.](/wiki/Undocumented_immigrant_population_of_the_United_States "Undocumented immigrant population of the United States"),[[135]](#cite_note-135) and criticized [birthright citizenship](/wiki/Birthright_citizenship_in_the_United_States "Birthright citizenship in the United States") for incentivizing "[anchor babies](/wiki/Anchor_babies "Anchor babies")".[[136]](#cite_note-136) According to an analysis in *[Political Science Quarterly](/wiki/Political_Science_Quarterly "Political Science Quarterly")*, Trump made "explicitly racist and sexist appeals to win over white voters" during his 2016 presidential campaign.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchaffnerMacwilliamsNteta201831-137) In particular, his campaign launch speech drew criticism for claiming Mexican immigrants were "bringing drugs, they're bringing crime, they're rapists";[[138]](#cite_note-138) in response, NBC fired him from *Celebrity Apprentice*.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

Trump's FEC-required reports listed assets above $1.4 billion and outstanding debts of at least $265 million.[[140]](#cite_note-disclosure-140)[[141]](#cite_note-141) He did not release [his tax returns](/wiki/Tax_returns_of_Donald_Trump "Tax returns of Donald Trump"), contrary to the practice of every major candidate since 1976 and to promises he made in 2014 and 2015 to release them if he ran for office.[[142]](#cite_note-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143) He said his tax returns were being [audited](/wiki/Income_tax_audit "Income tax audit"), and that his lawyers had advised him against releasing them.[[144]](#cite_note-144) After a lengthy court battle to block release of his tax returns and other records to the [Manhattan district attorney](/wiki/Manhattan_district_attorney "Manhattan district attorney") for a criminal investigation, including two appeals by Trump to the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court"), in February 2021 the high court allowed the records to be released to the prosecutor for review by a grand jury.[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146) In October 2016, portions of Trump's state filings for 1995 were leaked to a reporter from *The New York Times*. They show that he had declared a loss of $916 million that year, which could have let him avoid taxes for up to 18 years.[[147]](#cite_note-147)

Trump won the election with 306 pledged [electoral votes](/wiki/Electoral_College_(United_States) "Electoral College (United States)") versus 232 for Democratic candidate [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton"). After elector [defections on both sides](/wiki/Faithless_electors_in_the_2016_United_States_presidential_election "Faithless electors in the 2016 United States presidential election"), the official count was 304 to 227.[[148]](#cite_note-148) The fifth person to be elected president [despite losing the popular vote](/wiki/List_of_United_States_presidential_elections_in_which_the_winner_lost_the_popular_vote "List of United States presidential elections in which the winner lost the popular vote"),[[a]](#cite_note-electoral-college-149) he received nearly 2.9 million fewer votes than Clinton, 46.3% to her 48.25%.[[149]](#cite_note-150) He was the only president who neither served in the military nor held any government office prior to becoming president.[[150]](#cite_note-151) His election marked the return of a Republican [undivided government](/wiki/Divided_government_in_the_United_States "Divided government in the United States").[[b]](#cite_note-152)[[151]](#cite_note-153) Trump's victory [sparked protests](/wiki/Protests_against_Donald_Trump#Before_first_presidency "Protests against Donald Trump") in major U.S. cities.[[152]](#cite_note-154)[[153]](#cite_note-155)

## First presidency (2017–2021)

Main article: [First presidency of Donald Trump](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "First presidency of Donald Trump")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of the Donald Trump presidencies § First presidency (2017–2021)](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_Donald_Trump_presidencies#First_presidency_(2017–2021) "Timeline of the Donald Trump presidencies").

[![Trump, with his family watching, raises his right hand and places his left hand on the Bible as he takes the oath of office. Roberts stands opposite him administering the oath](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Donald_Trump_swearing_in_ceremony.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_swearing_in_ceremony.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_swearing_in_ceremony.jpg)

Taking the [oath of office](/wiki/Oath_of_office_of_the_President_of_the_United_States "Oath of office of the President of the United States"), administered by [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States") [John Roberts](/wiki/John_Roberts "John Roberts"), January 20, 2017

Trump [was inaugurated](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Donald_Trump "First inauguration of Donald Trump") on January 20, 2017. The day after his inauguration, an estimated 2.6 million people worldwide, including 500,000 in Washington, D.C., protested against him in the [Women's Marches](/wiki/2017_Women%27s_March "2017 Women's March").[[154]](#cite_note-156) During his first two weeks in office, Trump signed [eighteen executive orders](/wiki/List_of_executive_orders_in_the_first_Trump_presidency "List of executive orders in the first Trump presidency"), including authorizing procedures for repealing the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act ("Obamacare"), withdrawal from the Trans-Pacific Partnership negotiations, advancement of the [Keystone XL](/wiki/Keystone_Pipeline "Keystone Pipeline") and [Dakota Access Pipeline](/wiki/Dakota_Access_Pipeline "Dakota Access Pipeline") projects, and planning for a wall along the U.S. border with Mexico.[[155]](#cite_note-157)

### Conflicts of interest

See also: [First presidency of Donald Trump § Ethics](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump#Ethics "First presidency of Donald Trump")

Trump continued to profit from his businesses during his first presidency and knew how his administration's policies affected them.[[156]](#cite_note-BBC041817-158)[[157]](#cite_note-Venook-159) Although he said he would eschew "new foreign deals", the Trump Organization pursued operational expansions in Scotland, Dubai, and the Dominican Republic.[[156]](#cite_note-BBC041817-158)[[157]](#cite_note-Venook-159) Lobbyists, foreign government officials, and Trump donors and allies generated hundreds of millions of dollars for his resorts and hotels.[[158]](#cite_note-160)

### Domestic policy

Main articles: [Domestic policy of the first Trump administration](/wiki/Domestic_policy_of_the_first_Trump_administration "Domestic policy of the first Trump administration"), [Economic policy of the first Trump administration](/wiki/Economic_policy_of_the_first_Trump_administration "Economic policy of the first Trump administration"), [Environmental policy of the first Trump administration](/wiki/Environmental_policy_of_the_first_Trump_administration "Environmental policy of the first Trump administration"), and [Social policy of the first Trump administration](/wiki/Social_policy_of_the_first_Trump_administration "Social policy of the first Trump administration")

Trump took office at the height of the longest [economic expansion](/wiki/Economic_expansion "Economic expansion") in American history,[[159]](#cite_note-VanDam-161) which began in 2009 and continued until February 2020, when the [COVID-19 recession](/wiki/COVID-19_recession "COVID-19 recession") began.[[160]](#cite_note-162) In December 2017, he signed the [Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017](/wiki/Tax_Cuts_and_Jobs_Act_of_2017 "Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017"), which reduced tax rates for businesses and individuals and eliminated the penalty associated with the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act")'s individual mandate.[[161]](#cite_note-163)[[162]](#cite_note-164) Under Trump, the federal budget deficit increased by almost 50 percent, to nearly $1 trillion in 2019.[[163]](#cite_note-165) By the end of his term, the [U.S. national debt](/wiki/U.S._national_debt "U.S. national debt") increased by 39 percent, reaching $27.75 trillion, and the U.S. [debt-to-GDP ratio](/wiki/Debt-to-GDP_ratio "Debt-to-GDP ratio") hit a post-World War II high.[[164]](#cite_note-166)

Trump rejects the [scientific consensus on climate change](/wiki/Scientific_consensus_on_climate_change "Scientific consensus on climate change").[[165]](#cite_note-167)
He reduced the budget for [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_the_United_States#Renewable_energy_research "Renewable energy in the United States") research by 40 percent and reversed Obama-era policies directed at curbing [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[166]](#cite_note-168) He [withdrew from the Paris Agreement](/wiki/United_States_and_the_Paris_Agreement "United States and the Paris Agreement"), making the U.S. the only nation to not ratify it.[[167]](#cite_note-169)

Trump aimed and aims to boost the production and exports of [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel").[[168]](#cite_note-170)[[169]](#cite_note-171) [Natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") expanded under Trump, but [coal continued to decline](/wiki/Coal_mining_in_the_United_States#Production "Coal mining in the United States").[[170]](#cite_note-172)[[171]](#cite_note-Subramaniam-173)
He [rolled back](/wiki/Rollback "Rollback") more than 100 federal environmental regulations, including those that curbed [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions"), air and [water pollution](/wiki/Water_pollution "Water pollution"), and the use of toxic substances. He weakened protections for animals and [environmental standards](/wiki/Environmental_standard#United_States "Environmental standard") for federal infrastructure projects, and expanded permitted areas for drilling and [resource extraction](/wiki/Resource_extraction "Resource extraction"), such as allowing [drilling in the Arctic Refuge](/wiki/Arctic_Refuge_drilling_controversy "Arctic Refuge drilling controversy").[[172]](#cite_note-174)

Trump dismantled federal regulations on health,[[173]](#cite_note-175)[[174]](#cite_note-midnight-176) labor,[[174]](#cite_note-midnight-176) the environment,[[175]](#cite_note-177)[[174]](#cite_note-midnight-176) and other areas, including a bill that revoked the Obama-era regulation restricting the sale of firearms to severely mentally ill people.[[176]](#cite_note-178) During his first six weeks in office, he delayed, suspended, or reversed ninety federal regulations,[[177]](#cite_note-179) often "after requests by the regulated industries".[[178]](#cite_note-180)

Trump vowed to repeal and replace the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act").[[179]](#cite_note-181) He scaled back the act's implementation through executive orders.[[180]](#cite_note-182)[[181]](#cite_note-183) He expressed a desire to "let Obamacare fail"; his administration halved the [enrollment period](/wiki/Annual_enrollment "Annual enrollment") and drastically reduced funding for enrollment promotion.[[182]](#cite_note-184)[[183]](#cite_note-185)

In response to the [opioid epidemic](/wiki/Opioid_epidemic_in_the_United_States "Opioid epidemic in the United States"), Trump signed legislation in 2018 to increase funding for drug treatments, but was widely criticized for failing to make a concrete strategy.[[184]](#cite_note-186) He barred organizations that provide abortions or abortion referrals from receiving federal funds.[[185]](#cite_note-187) His administration rolled back key components of the Obama administration's workplace protections against [discrimination of LGBTQ people](/wiki/Discrimination_against_LGBT_people "Discrimination against LGBT people").[[186]](#cite_note-188) His attempted rollback of anti-discrimination protections for [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") patients in August 2020 was halted by a federal judge after a Supreme Court ruling extended employees' civil rights protections to [gender identity](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity") and sexual orientation.[[187]](#cite_note-189) His administration [took an anti-marijuana position](/wiki/Cannabis_policy_of_the_Donald_Trump_administration "Cannabis policy of the Donald Trump administration"), revoking [Obama-era policies](/wiki/Cole_Memorandum "Cole Memorandum") that provided protections for states that legalized marijuana.[[188]](#cite_note-190) Trump is a long-time advocate of capital punishment,[[189]](#cite_note-191)[[190]](#cite_note-192) and his administration oversaw the [federal government execute](/wiki/Capital_punishment_by_the_United_States_federal_government "Capital punishment by the United States federal government") 13 prisoners, more than in the previous 56 years combined, ending a 17-year moratorium.[[191]](#cite_note-193)

### Pardons and commutations

Further information: [List of people granted executive clemency in the first Trump presidency](/wiki/List_of_people_granted_executive_clemency_in_the_first_Trump_presidency "List of people granted executive clemency in the first Trump presidency")

During his first term, Trump granted 237 requests for clemency, fewer than all presidents since 1900 with the exception of [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") and [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush").[[192]](#cite_note-194) Only 25 of them had been vetted by the Justice Department's [Office of the Pardon Attorney](/wiki/Office_of_the_Pardon_Attorney "Office of the Pardon Attorney"); the others were granted to people with personal or political connections to him, his family, and his allies, or recommended by celebrities.[[193]](#cite_note-road-195)[[194]](#cite_note-OloDaw-196) In his last full day in office, he granted 73 pardons and commuted 70 sentences.[[195]](#cite_note-197) The pardons of three military service members convicted of or charged with violent crimes were opposed by military leaders.[[196]](#cite_note-198)

### Immigration

Main article: [Immigration policy of the first Trump administration](/wiki/Immigration_policy_of_the_first_Trump_administration "Immigration policy of the first Trump administration")

Further information: [Mexico–United States border crisis § First Trump administration (2017–2021)](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_crisis#First_Trump_administration_(2017–2021) "Mexico–United States border crisis")

As president, Trump described illegal immigration as an "invasion" of the United States[[197]](#cite_note-199) and drastically escalated immigration enforcement.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson2017a-200)[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnsonCuison-Villazor2019-201) He implemented harsh policies against asylum seekers[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnsonCuison-Villazor2019-201) and [deployed nearly 6,000 troops](/wiki/Operation_Faithful_Patriot "Operation Faithful Patriot") to the [U.S.–Mexico border](/wiki/U.S.%E2%80%93Mexico_border "U.S.–Mexico border") to stop illegal crossings.[[200]](#cite_note-202) He reduced the number of [refugees admitted](/wiki/United_States_Refugee_Admissions_Program "United States Refugee Admissions Program") to record lows, from an annual limit of 110,000 before he took office to 15,000 in 2021.[[201]](#cite_note-203)[[202]](#cite_note-204)[[203]](#cite_note-205) Trump also increased restrictions on granting [permanent residency](/wiki/Permanent_residency_in_the_United_States "Permanent residency in the United States") to [immigrants needing public benefits](/wiki/Public_charge_rule "Public charge rule").[[204]](#cite_note-206) One of his central campaign promises was to build a [wall along the U.S.–Mexico border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_wall "Mexico–United States border wall");[[205]](#cite_note-timm-207) during his first term, the U.S. built 73 miles (117 km) of wall in areas without barriers and 365 miles (587 km) to replace older barriers.[[206]](#cite_note-208) In 2018, Trump's refusal to sign any [spending bill](/wiki/Spending_bill "Spending bill") unless it allocated funding for the border wall[[207]](#cite_note-209) resulted in [the longest-ever federal government shutdown](/wiki/2018%E2%80%932019_United_States_federal_government_shutdown "2018–2019 United States federal government shutdown"), for 35 days from December 2018 to January 2019.[[208]](#cite_note-Gambino-210)[[209]](#cite_note-211) The shutdown ended after he agreed to fund the government without any funds for the wall.[[208]](#cite_note-Gambino-210) To avoid another shutdown, Congress passed a funding bill with $1.4 billion for border fencing in February.[[210]](#cite_note-Wilkie-212) Trump later declared a [national emergency on the southern border](/wiki/National_Emergency_Concerning_the_Southern_Border_of_the_United_States "National Emergency Concerning the Southern Border of the United States") to divert $6.1 billion of funding to the border wall[[210]](#cite_note-Wilkie-212) despite congressional disagreement.[[211]](#cite_note-213)

In January 2017, Trump signed [an executive order](/wiki/Executive_Order_13769 "Executive Order 13769") that [denied entry to citizens from six Muslim-majority countries](/wiki/Trump_travel_ban "Trump travel ban") for four months and from Syria indefinitely.[[212]](#cite_note-frontline-214)[[213]](#cite_note-airport-215) The order caused [many protests](/wiki/Protests_against_Executive_Order_13769 "Protests against Executive Order 13769") and [legal challenges](/wiki/Legal_challenges_to_the_Trump_travel_ban "Legal challenges to the Trump travel ban") that resulted in [nationwide injunctions](/wiki/Nationwide_injunction "Nationwide injunction").[[212]](#cite_note-frontline-214)[[213]](#cite_note-airport-215)[[214]](#cite_note-216) [A revised order](/wiki/Executive_Order_13780 "Executive Order 13780") giving some exceptions was also blocked by courts,[[215]](#cite_note-217)[[216]](#cite_note-218) but [the Supreme Court ruled in June](/wiki/International_Refugee_Assistance_Project_v._Trump "International Refugee Assistance Project v. Trump") that the ban could be enforced on those lacking "a *bona fide* relationship with a person or entity" in the U.S.[[217]](#cite_note-219) Trump replaced the ban in September with [a presidential proclamation](/wiki/Presidential_Proclamation_9645 "Presidential Proclamation 9645") extending travel bans to North Koreans, Chadians, and some Venezuelan officials, but excluded Iraq and Sudan.[[218]](#cite_note-220) The Supreme Court allowed that version to go into effect in December 2017,[[219]](#cite_note-221) and ultimately upheld the ban in 2019.[[220]](#cite_note-222)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Ursula_%28detention_center%29_2-res.jpg/250px-Ursula_%28detention_center%29_2-res.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ursula_(detention_center)_2-res.jpg)

Children, sleeping mats, and foil blankets in wire mesh compartment, [Ursula detention facility](/wiki/Ursula_detention_facility "Ursula detention facility"), June 2018

From 2017 to 2018, the Trump administration had [a policy of family separation](/wiki/Trump_administration_family_separation_policy "Trump administration family separation policy") that separated over 4,400 children, some as young as four months old,[[221]](#cite_note-tina_v-223) from migrant parents at the U.S.–Mexico border.[[222]](#cite_note-224)[[223]](#cite_note-Spagat-225) The unprecedented[[224]](#cite_note-Domonoske-226) policy sparked public outrage in the country.[[225]](#cite_note-227) Despite Trump initially blaming Democrats[[226]](#cite_note-228)[[227]](#cite_note-229) and insisting he could not stop the policy with an executive order, he acceded to public pressure in June 2018 and mandated that illegal immigrant families be detained together unless "there is a concern" of risk for the child.[[228]](#cite_note-230)[[229]](#cite_note-231) A judge later ordered that the families be reunited and further separations stopped except in limited circumstances,[[230]](#cite_note-232)[[231]](#cite_note-233) though over 1,000 additional children were separated from their families after the order.[[223]](#cite_note-Spagat-225) By April 2024, 1,360 children had not been confirmed as reunified with their families.[[221]](#cite_note-tina_v-223)

### Foreign policy

Main articles: [Foreign policy of the first Trump administration](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_the_first_Trump_administration "Foreign policy of the first Trump administration") and [Tariffs in the first Trump administration](/wiki/Tariffs_in_the_first_Trump_administration "Tariffs in the first Trump administration")

Further information: [Russia–United States relations § First Trump administration (2017–2021)](/wiki/Russia%E2%80%93United_States_relations#First_Trump_administration_(2017–2021) "Russia–United States relations"), [China–United States relations § First Trump administration (2017–2021)](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_relations#First_Trump_administration_(2017–2021) "China–United States relations"), [2017–2018 North Korea crisis](/wiki/2017%E2%80%932018_North_Korea_crisis "2017–2018 North Korea crisis"), and [2018–19 Korean peace process](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Korean_peace_process "2018–19 Korean peace process")

[![A group of seven men and one woman, sitting at a round conference table. Trump wears a dark blue suit, white dress shirt, and light blue necktie. A small sign reading "G7 France Biarritz 2019" hangs on the wall behind them.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/-G7Biarritz_%2848616362963%29.jpg/250px--G7Biarritz_%2848616362963%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:-G7Biarritz_(48616362963).jpg)

[G7](/wiki/Group_of_Seven "Group of Seven") leaders at the [45th summit](/wiki/45th_G7_summit "45th G7 summit") in France, 2019

Trump describes himself as a "nationalist"[[232]](#cite_note-234) and his foreign policy as "[America First](/wiki/America_First "America First")".[[233]](#cite_note-Bennhold-235) He supported [populist](/wiki/Populist "Populist"), [neo-nationalist](/wiki/Neo-nationalist "Neo-nationalist"), and authoritarian governments.[[234]](#cite_note-236) Unpredictability, uncertainty, and inconsistency characterized foreign relations during his tenure.[[233]](#cite_note-Bennhold-235)[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGurk2020-237) Relations between the U.S. and its European allies were strained under Trump.[[236]](#cite_note-238) He criticized [NATO allies](/wiki/Member_states_of_NATO "Member states of NATO") and privately suggested that the U.S. should [withdraw from NATO](/wiki/Withdrawal_from_NATO#United_States "Withdrawal from NATO").[[237]](#cite_note-239)[[238]](#cite_note-240)

Trump supported many of the policies of Israeli prime minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu").[[239]](#cite_note-241) In 2020, Trump hosted the signing of the [Abraham Accords](/wiki/Abraham_Accords "Abraham Accords") between Israel and the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") and [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain") to normalize their foreign relations.[[240]](#cite_note-242)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/President_Trump_%26_the_First_Lady%27s_Trip_to_Europe_%2842547210635%29.jpg/250px-President_Trump_%26_the_First_Lady%27s_Trip_to_Europe_%2842547210635%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Trump_%26_the_First_Lady%27s_Trip_to_Europe_(42547210635).jpg)

Shaking hands with Russian president Vladimir Putin during the [2018 summit](/wiki/2018_Russia%E2%80%93United_States_Summit "2018 Russia–United States Summit") in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), Finland

Trump began [a trade war with China](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war "China–United States trade war") in 2018 after imposing tariffs and other trade barriers he said would force China to end longstanding unfair trade practice and [intellectual property infringement](/wiki/Allegations_of_intellectual_property_infringement_by_China "Allegations of intellectual property infringement by China").[[241]](#cite_note-243) Trump weakened the toughest U.S. sanctions imposed after the [2014 Russian annexation of Crimea](/wiki/2014_Russian_annexation_of_Crimea "2014 Russian annexation of Crimea").[[242]](#cite_note-244)[[243]](#cite_note-245) Trump praised and, according to some critics, rarely criticized Russian president [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin"),[[244]](#cite_note-246)[[245]](#cite_note-247) though he opposed some actions of Russia's government.[[246]](#cite_note-248) He withdrew the U.S. from the [Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty](/wiki/Intermediate-Range_Nuclear_Forces_Treaty "Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty"), citing alleged Russian noncompliance,[[247]](#cite_note-249) and supported Russia's possible return to the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7").[[248]](#cite_note-G8-250)

As [North Korea's nuclear weapons](/wiki/North_Korea%27s_nuclear_weapons "North Korea's nuclear weapons") were increasingly seen as a serious threat,[[249]](#cite_note-251) Trump became the first sitting U.S. president to meet a North Korean leader, meeting [Kim Jong Un](/wiki/Kim_Jong_Un "Kim Jong Un") three times between June 2018 and June 2019.[[250]](#cite_note-252) Talks on North Korean [denuclearization](/wiki/Denuclearization "Denuclearization") broke down in October 2019, and no agreement was reached.[[251]](#cite_note-253)[[252]](#cite_note-254)

### Personnel

Main articles: [Political appointments of the first Trump administration](/wiki/Political_appointments_of_the_first_Trump_administration "Political appointments of the first Trump administration") and [First cabinet of Donald Trump](/wiki/First_cabinet_of_Donald_Trump "First cabinet of Donald Trump")

Further information: [Government hiring and personnel of Donald Trump](/wiki/Government_hiring_and_personnel_of_Donald_Trump "Government hiring and personnel of Donald Trump")

By the end of Trump's first year in office, 34 percent of his original staff had resigned, been fired, or been reassigned.[[253]](#cite_note-255) By July 2018, 61 percent of his senior aides had left[[254]](#cite_note-256) and 141 staffers had left in the previous year.[[255]](#cite_note-257) Both figures set a record for recent presidents.[[256]](#cite_note-Keith-258) Various close personal aides to Trump stopped working for him or were forced to leave.[[257]](#cite_note-Brookings-259) Trump publicly disparaged several of his former top officials.[[258]](#cite_note-260)

Trump had four [White House chiefs of staff](/wiki/White_House_chiefs_of_staff "White House chiefs of staff"), marginalizing or pushing out several.[[259]](#cite_note-Keither-261) In May 2017, he [dismissed Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) director James Comey](/wiki/Dismissal_of_James_Comey "Dismissal of James Comey"), saying a few days later that he was concerned about Comey's role in the Trump–Russia investigations.[[260]](#cite_note-262)[[261]](#cite_note-263) Three of Trump's 15 original cabinet members left or were forced to resign within his first year.[[262]](#cite_note-538_Cabinet-264)[[257]](#cite_note-Brookings-259) Trump was slow to appoint second-tier officials in the executive branch, saying many of the positions are unnecessary. In October 2017, there were hundreds of sub-cabinet positions without a nominee.[[263]](#cite_note-265) By January 8, 2019, of 706 key positions, 433 had been filled and he had no nominee for 264.[[264]](#cite_note-266)

### Judiciary

Further information: [List of federal judges appointed by Donald Trump](/wiki/List_of_federal_judges_appointed_by_Donald_Trump "List of federal judges appointed by Donald Trump") and [Donald Trump judicial appointment controversies](/wiki/Donald_Trump_judicial_appointment_controversies "Donald Trump judicial appointment controversies")

Trump appointed 234 [federal judges](/wiki/United_States_federal_judge "United States federal judge"), including 54 to the [courts of appeals](/wiki/United_States_courts_of_appeals "United States courts of appeals") and [three](/wiki/Donald_Trump_Supreme_Court_candidates "Donald Trump Supreme Court candidates") to the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"): [Neil Gorsuch](/wiki/Neil_Gorsuch "Neil Gorsuch"), [Brett Kavanaugh](/wiki/Brett_Kavanaugh "Brett Kavanaugh"), and [Amy Coney Barrett](/wiki/Amy_Coney_Barrett "Amy Coney Barrett"),[[265]](#cite_note-267)[[266]](#cite_note-268) which politically shifted the Court to the right.[[267]](#cite_note-269)[[268]](#cite_note-270)[[269]](#cite_note-271) In the 2016 campaign, he pledged that *[Roe v. Wade](/wiki/Roe_v._Wade "Roe v. Wade")* would be overturned "automatically" if he were elected and given the opportunity to appoint two or three anti-abortion justices. He later took credit when *Roe* was overturned by *[Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization](/wiki/Dobbs_v._Jackson_Women%27s_Health_Organization "Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization")* in 2022; all three of his Supreme Court nominees voted with the majority.[[270]](#cite_note-272)[[271]](#cite_note-273) Trump disparaged courts and judges he disagreed with, often in personal terms, and questioned the judiciary's constitutional authority. His attacks on courts drew rebukes from observers, including sitting federal judges, concerned about the effect of his statements on the [judicial independence](/wiki/Judicial_independence "Judicial independence") and public confidence in the judiciary.[[272]](#cite_note-274)[[273]](#cite_note-275)

### COVID-19 pandemic

Main article: [COVID-19 pandemic in the United States](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States")

Further information: [U.S. federal government response to the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/U.S._federal_government_response_to_the_COVID-19_pandemic "U.S. federal government response to the COVID-19 pandemic") and [Communication of the Trump administration during the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Communication_of_the_Trump_administration_during_the_COVID-19_pandemic "Communication of the Trump administration during the COVID-19 pandemic")

See also: [Economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic in the United States](/wiki/Economic_impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "Economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic in the United States")

[![Trump speaks in the West Wing briefing room with various officials standing behind him, all in formal attire and without face masks](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/White_House_Press_Briefing_%2849666120807%29.jpg/250px-White_House_Press_Briefing_%2849666120807%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:White_House_Press_Briefing_(49666120807).jpg)

Conducting a COVID-19 press briefing with members of the [White House Coronavirus Task Force](/wiki/White_House_Coronavirus_Task_Force "White House Coronavirus Task Force") on March 15, 2020

After ignoring public health warnings and calls for action from health officials within his administration for weeks,[[274]](#cite_note-276) Trump established the [White House Coronavirus Task Force](/wiki/White_House_Coronavirus_Task_Force "White House Coronavirus Task Force") in late January.[[275]](#cite_note-277) In March, he signed into law the [CARES Act](/wiki/CARES_Act "CARES Act")—a $2.2 trillion bipartisan [economic stimulus](/wiki/Economic_stimulus "Economic stimulus") bill—the largest stimulus in U.S. history.[[276]](#cite_note-NY_Times_stimulus_united_senate-278)[[277]](#cite_note-AP_Trump_signs_2.2-279) After weeks of attacks to draw attention away from his slow response, Trump halted funding of the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") in April.[[278]](#cite_note-280) He encouraged right-wing [protests against social-distancing policies](/wiki/COVID-19_protests_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 protests in the United States") in states with stay-at-home orders[[279]](#cite_note-281) and repeatedly pressured federal health agencies to take actions he favored,[[280]](#cite_note-CNN-testing-pressure-282) such as approving unproven treatments.[[281]](#cite_note-283)[[282]](#cite_note-pressed-284)

In October, Trump was [hospitalized for three days](/wiki/White_House_COVID-19_outbreak#Presidential_hospitalization_and_early_release "White House COVID-19 outbreak") with a severe case of [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") while his medical team downplayed the severity of his condition.[[283]](#cite_note-285)

### Investigations

Further information: [Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections](/wiki/Russian_interference_in_the_2016_United_States_elections "Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections"), [Mueller special counsel investigation](/wiki/Mueller_special_counsel_investigation "Mueller special counsel investigation"), and [Mueller report](/wiki/Mueller_report "Mueller report")

After he assumed office, Trump was the subject of increasing [Justice Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") and congressional scrutiny, with investigations covering his election campaign, transition, and inauguration, actions taken during his presidency, [his private businesses](/wiki/The_Trump_Organization "The Trump Organization"), personal taxes, and [his charitable foundation](/wiki/Donald_J._Trump_Foundation "Donald J. Trump Foundation").[[284]](#cite_note-286) There were ten federal criminal investigations, eight state and local investigations, and twelve congressional investigations.[[285]](#cite_note-287)

In July 2016, the [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") launched [Crossfire Hurricane](/wiki/Crossfire_Hurricane_(FBI_investigation) "Crossfire Hurricane (FBI investigation)"), an investigation into possible links between Russia and Trump's 2016 campaign.[[286]](#cite_note-288) After Trump fired Comey in May 2017, the FBI opened a second investigation into Trump's personal and [business dealings with Russia](/wiki/Business_projects_of_Donald_Trump_in_Russia "Business projects of Donald Trump in Russia").[[287]](#cite_note-289) In January 2017, three U.S. intelligence agencies jointly stated with "high confidence" that [Russia interfered in the 2016 presidential election](/wiki/Russian_interference_in_the_2016_United_States_elections "Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections") to favor Trump.[[288]](#cite_note-290)[[289]](#cite_note-291) Crossfire Hurricane was later transferred to [Robert Mueller](/wiki/Robert_Mueller "Robert Mueller")'s [special counsel investigation](/wiki/Mueller_special_counsel_investigation "Mueller special counsel investigation");[[290]](#cite_note-292) the investigation into Trump's ties to Russia was ended by Deputy Attorney General [Rod Rosenstein](/wiki/Rod_Rosenstein "Rod Rosenstein") after he told the FBI that Mueller would pursue the matter.[[291]](#cite_note-never-293)[[292]](#cite_note-294) At the request of Rosenstein, the Mueller investigation examined criminal matters "in connection with Russia's 2016 election interference".[[291]](#cite_note-never-293) [Mueller's final report](/wiki/Mueller_report "Mueller report") in March 2019[[293]](#cite_note-295) found that Russia interfered in 2016 to favor Trump[[294]](#cite_note-296) and that Trump and his campaign welcomed and encouraged the effort,[[295]](#cite_note-297)[[296]](#cite_note-298)[[297]](#cite_note-299) but that the evidence "did not establish" that Trump campaign members conspired or coordinated with Russia.[[298]](#cite_note-300)[[299]](#cite_note-takeaways-301) Trump claimed the report exonerated him despite Mueller writing that it did not.[[300]](#cite_note-302) The report also detailed potential obstruction of justice by Trump but "did not draw ultimate conclusions"[[301]](#cite_note-303)[[302]](#cite_note-304) and left the decision to charge the laws to Congress.[[303]](#cite_note-LM-305)

### Impeachments

Main articles: [First impeachment of Donald Trump](/wiki/First_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "First impeachment of Donald Trump") and [Second impeachment of Donald Trump](/wiki/Second_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "Second impeachment of Donald Trump")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/President_Trump_Delivers_Remarks_%2849498772251%29.jpg/250px-President_Trump_Delivers_Remarks_%2849498772251%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Trump_Delivers_Remarks_(49498772251).jpg)

Displaying the headline "Trump acquitted" in 2020

Trump was [impeached](/wiki/Impeachment_in_the_United_States "Impeachment in the United States") twice by the House of Representatives, though acquitted by the Senate on both occasions. The [first impeachment](/wiki/First_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "First impeachment of Donald Trump") arose from a whistleblower complaint that in July 2019 Trump [had pressured](/wiki/2019_Trump%E2%80%93Ukraine_scandal "2019 Trump–Ukraine scandal") Ukrainian president [Volodymyr Zelenskyy](/wiki/Volodymyr_Zelenskyy "Volodymyr Zelenskyy") to investigate Joe and Hunter Biden,[[304]](#cite_note-undermine-306) in an attempt to gain an advantage in the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election").[[305]](#cite_note-307) In December 2019, the House voted to impeach Trump for [abuse of power](/wiki/Abuse_of_power "Abuse of power") and [obstruction of Congress](/wiki/Obstruction_of_Congress "Obstruction of Congress"),[[306]](#cite_note-308) and the Senate acquitted him in February 2020.[[307]](#cite_note-309)

The [second impeachment](/wiki/Second_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "Second impeachment of Donald Trump") came after the January 6 Capitol attack, for which the House charged Trump with [incitement of insurrection](/wiki/Incitement_of_insurrection "Incitement of insurrection") on January 13, 2021.[[308]](#cite_note-SecondImpeachment-310) Trump left office on January 20, and was acquitted on February 13. Seven Republican senators voted for conviction.[[309]](#cite_note-311)

### 2020 presidential election and rejection of outcome
