Further information: [2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"), [Donald Trump 2020 presidential campaign](/wiki/Donald_Trump_2020_presidential_campaign "Donald Trump 2020 presidential campaign"), and [Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/Attempts_to_overturn_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election "Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election")

Trump filed to run for reelection only a few hours after becoming president in 2017.[[310]](#cite_note-312) He held his first reelection rally less than a month after taking office[[311]](#cite_note-313) and officially became the [Republican nominee](/wiki/2020_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "2020 Republican Party presidential primaries") in August 2020.[[312]](#cite_note-314) Trump's campaign focused on crime, claiming that cities would descend into lawlessness if Democratic nominee Joe Biden won.[[313]](#cite_note-315) Starting in early 2020, Trump sowed doubts about the election, claiming without evidence that it would be rigged and that widespread use of mail balloting would produce massive election fraud.[[314]](#cite_note-316)[[315]](#cite_note-317) He repeatedly refused to say whether he would accept the results if he lost and commit to a [peaceful transition of power](/wiki/Peaceful_transition_of_power "Peaceful transition of power").[[316]](#cite_note-318)[[317]](#cite_note-319)

Joe Biden won the November 2020 election, receiving 81.3 million votes (51.3 percent) to Trump's 74.2 million (46.8 percent)[[318]](#cite_note-vote1-320)[[319]](#cite_note-vote2-321) and 306 electoral votes to Trump's 232,[[320]](#cite_note-formalize-322) with the [Electoral College](/wiki/United_States_Electoral_College "United States Electoral College") formalizing Biden's victory on December 14.[[320]](#cite_note-formalize-322) Trump declared victory before the results were known on the morning after the election.[[321]](#cite_note-323) Days later, when Biden was projected the winner, Trump baselessly alleged election fraud.[[322]](#cite_note-324) As part of [an effort to overturn the results](/wiki/Attempts_to_overturn_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election "Attempts to overturn the 2020 United States presidential election"), Trump and his allies filed many [lawsuits challenging the results](/wiki/Post-election_lawsuits_related_to_the_2020_U.S._presidential_election "Post-election lawsuits related to the 2020 U.S. presidential election"), which were rejected by at least 86 judges in both state and federal courts for having no factual or legal basis.[[323]](#cite_note-325)[[324]](#cite_note-326)

Trump's allegations were also refuted by state election officials,[[325]](#cite_note-327) and the Supreme Court declined to hear [a case asking it to overturn the results](/wiki/Texas_v._Pennsylvania "Texas v. Pennsylvania") in four states won by Biden.[[326]](#cite_note-328) Trump repeatedly sought help to overturn the results, personally pressuring Republican local and state office-holders,[[327]](#cite_note-amyG-329) Republican legislators,[[328]](#cite_note-pressure-330) the Justice Department,[[329]](#cite_note-331) and Vice President Pence,[[330]](#cite_note-332) urging actions such as [replacing presidential electors](/wiki/Trump_fake_electors_plot "Trump fake electors plot"),[[328]](#cite_note-pressure-330) or that Georgia officials ["find" votes and announce](/wiki/Trump%E2%80%93Raffensperger_phone_call "Trump–Raffensperger phone call") a "recalculated" result.[[327]](#cite_note-amyG-329) Trump did not attend [Biden's inauguration](/wiki/Biden%27s_inauguration "Biden's inauguration") on January 20.[[331]](#cite_note-333)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/2021_storming_of_the_United_States_Capitol_DSC09254-2_%2850820534063%29_%28retouched%29.jpg/250px-2021_storming_of_the_United_States_Capitol_DSC09254-2_%2850820534063%29_%28retouched%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2021_storming_of_the_United_States_Capitol_DSC09254-2_(50820534063)_(retouched).jpg)

A crowd of Trump supporters during the attack

### January 6 attack

Further information: [January 6 United States Capitol attack](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack")

On January 6, 2021, while [Congress was certifying the presidential election results](/wiki/2021_United_States_Electoral_College_vote_count "2021 United States Electoral College vote count"), [Trump held a rally](/wiki/January_6_Trump_rally "January 6 Trump rally") at which he called for the election to be overturned and urged his supporters to "fight like hell" and "take back our country" by marching to the Capitol.[[332]](#cite_note-334) His supporters then [formed a mob that broke into the building](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack"), disrupting certification and causing the evacuation of Congress.[[333]](#cite_note-335) More than 140 police officers were injured, and five people died during or after the attack.[[334]](#cite_note-336)[[335]](#cite_note-337) The event has been described as an attempted [self-coup](/wiki/Self-coup "Self-coup") by Trump.[[336]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPion-BerlinBruneauGoetze2022-338)[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarvey20223-339)[[338]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECastañedaJenks2023246-340) Congress later reconvened and confirmed Biden's victory in the early hours of January 7.[[339]](#cite_note-341)

## Between presidencies (2021–2025)

Upon leaving the White House, Trump began living at Mar-a-Lago, establishing an office there as provided for by the [Former Presidents Act](/wiki/Former_Presidents_Act "Former Presidents Act").[[340]](#cite_note-342) His continuing [false claims concerning the 2020 election](/wiki/Big_lie#Donald_Trump's_false_claims_of_a_stolen_election "Big lie") were commonly referred to as the "[big lie](/wiki/Big_lie "Big lie")" by his critics, although in May 2021, he and many of his supporters began using the term to refer to the election itself.[[341]](#cite_note-343)[[342]](#cite_note-key2-344) The Republican Party used his false claims about the election to justify [imposing new voting restrictions](/wiki/Republican_efforts_to_restrict_voting_following_the_2020_United_States_presidential_election "Republican efforts to restrict voting following the 2020 United States presidential election") in its favor.[[343]](#cite_note-345)[[344]](#cite_note-346)[[345]](#cite_note-347) He continued to pressure state legislators to overturn the election.[[346]](#cite_note-348) Unlike other former presidents, Trump continued to dominate his party; a 2022 profile in *The New York Times* described him as a modern [party boss](/wiki/Party_boss "Party boss").[[347]](#cite_note-PB2-349) He continued fundraising, raising a [war chest](/wiki/War_chest "War chest") containing more than twice that of the Republican Party, and profited from fundraisers many Republican candidates held at Mar-a-Lago. Much of his focus was on party governance and installing in key posts officials loyal to him.[[347]](#cite_note-PB2-349) In the [2022 midterm elections](/wiki/2022_midterm_elections "2022 midterm elections"), Trump endorsed over 200 candidates for various offices.[[348]](#cite_note-350) In February 2022, his [Trump Media & Technology Group](/wiki/Trump_Media_%26_Technology_Group "Trump Media & Technology Group") (TMTG) launched a pro-Trump social media platform called [Truth Social](/wiki/Truth_Social "Truth Social").[[349]](#cite_note-351) In March 2024, TMTG became a [public company](/wiki/Public_company "Public company").[[350]](#cite_note-352)

### Legal issues

See also: [Personal and business legal affairs of Donald Trump](/wiki/Personal_and_business_legal_affairs_of_Donald_Trump "Personal and business legal affairs of Donald Trump") and [Legal affairs of the first Trump presidency](/wiki/Legal_affairs_of_the_first_Trump_presidency "Legal affairs of the first Trump presidency")

In 2019, journalist [E. Jean Carroll](/wiki/E._Jean_Carroll "E. Jean Carroll") accused Trump of raping her in the 1990s and [sued him for defamation](/wiki/E._Jean_Carroll_v._Donald_J._Trump "E. Jean Carroll v. Donald J. Trump") over his denial.[[351]](#cite_note-353) Carroll sued him again in 2022 for [battery](/wiki/Battery_(crime) "Battery (crime)") and further defamation.[[352]](#cite_note-354) He was [found liable](/wiki/Legal_liability "Legal liability") for sexual abuse and defamation and ordered to pay $5 million in one case[[353]](#cite_note-355) and $83.3 million in the other.[[354]](#cite_note-uphold-356) Federal appeals courts upheld both findings and awards in December 2024 and September 2025, respectively.[[355]](#cite_note-357)[[354]](#cite_note-uphold-356)

In 2022, [New York filed a civil lawsuit](/wiki/New_York_business_fraud_lawsuit_against_the_Trump_Organization "New York business fraud lawsuit against the Trump Organization") against Trump accusing him of inflating the Trump Organization's value to gain an advantage with lenders and banks.[[356]](#cite_note-358)[[357]](#cite_note-TF-359) He was found liable and ordered to pay nearly $355 million plus interest.[[357]](#cite_note-TF-359) In August 2025, the appeals court upheld his liability and nonmonetary penalties, but voided the monetary penalty as excessive.[[358]](#cite_note-360)

In connection with Trump's efforts to overturn the 2020 election and his involvement in the January 6 attack, in December 2022 the [U.S. House committee on the attack](/wiki/United_States_House_Select_Committee_on_the_January_6_Attack "United States House Select Committee on the January 6 Attack") recommended criminal charges against him for [obstructing an official proceeding](/wiki/Obstructing_an_official_proceeding "Obstructing an official proceeding"), inciting or assisting an insurrection, and [conspiracy to defraud the United States](/wiki/Conspiracy_to_defraud_the_United_States "Conspiracy to defraud the United States").[[359]](#cite_note-361) In August 2023, a grand jury in [Fulton County, Georgia](/wiki/Fulton_County,_Georgia "Fulton County, Georgia"), indicted him on [13 charges, including racketeering](/wiki/Georgia_election_racketeering_prosecution "Georgia election racketeering prosecution"), for his efforts to subvert the 2020 election in the state.[[360]](#cite_note-362)[[361]](#cite_note-363) The case was dismissed in November 2025 after the new prosecutor declined to pursue the charges.[[362]](#cite_note-364)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Classified_intelligence_material_found_during_search_of_Mar-a-Lago.jpg/250px-Classified_intelligence_material_found_during_search_of_Mar-a-Lago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Classified_intelligence_material_found_during_search_of_Mar-a-Lago.jpg)

Classified intelligence material found during search of Mar-a-Lago

In January 2022, the [National Archives and Records Administration](/wiki/National_Archives_and_Records_Administration "National Archives and Records Administration") retrieved [15 boxes of documents](/wiki/Federal_prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_(classified_documents_case) "Federal prosecution of Donald Trump (classified documents case)") Trump had taken to [Mar-a-Lago](/wiki/Mar-a-Lago "Mar-a-Lago") after leaving the White House, some of which were classified.[[363]](#cite_note-cnn-tl222-365) In the ensuing Justice Department investigation, officials retrieved more classified documents from his lawyers.[[363]](#cite_note-cnn-tl222-365) On August 8, 2022, [FBI agents searched Mar-a-Lago](/wiki/FBI_search_of_Mar-a-Lago "FBI search of Mar-a-Lago") for illegally held documents, including those in breach of the [Espionage Act](/wiki/Espionage_Act "Espionage Act"), collecting 11 sets of classified documents, some marked top secret.[[364]](#cite_note-bddj0812222-366)[[365]](#cite_note-NYT-20220812222-367) A [federal grand jury](/wiki/Federal_grand_jury "Federal grand jury") constituted by Special Counsel [Jack Smith](/wiki/Jack_Smith_(lawyer) "Jack Smith (lawyer)") indicted Trump in June 2023 on 31 counts of "willfully retaining national defense information" under the Espionage Act, among other charges.[[363]](#cite_note-cnn-tl222-365)[[366]](#cite_note-368)[[367]](#cite_note-369) Trump pleaded not guilty.[[368]](#cite_note-370) In July 2024, judge [Aileen Cannon](/wiki/Aileen_Cannon "Aileen Cannon") dismissed the case, ruling Smith's appointment as special prosecutor was unconstitutional.[[369]](#cite_note-CD2-371) After his reelection, the [2020 election obstruction case](/wiki/Federal_prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_(election_obstruction_case) "Federal prosecution of Donald Trump (election obstruction case)") and the classified documents case were dismissed [without prejudice](/wiki/Prejudice_(legal_term)#With_prejudice_and_without_prejudice "Prejudice (legal term)") due to Justice Department policy against prosecuting sitting presidents.[[370]](#cite_note-dismiss2-372)

In May 2024, Trump [was convicted on 34 felony counts](/wiki/Prosecution_of_Donald_Trump_in_New_York "Prosecution of Donald Trump in New York") of [falsifying business records](/wiki/Falsifying_business_records "Falsifying business records").[[371]](#cite_note-conv-373) The case stemmed from evidence that he booked [Michael Cohen](/wiki/Michael_Cohen_(lawyer) "Michael Cohen (lawyer)")'s hush-money payments to adult film actress [Stormy Daniels](/wiki/Stormy_Daniels "Stormy Daniels") as business expenses to cover up his alleged 2006–2007 affair with Daniels during the 2016 election.[[371]](#cite_note-conv-373)[[372]](#cite_note-374) On January 10, 2025, the judge gave Trump a no-penalty sentence known as an unconditional discharge, saying that punitive requirements would have interfered with presidential immunity.[[373]](#cite_note-uds-375)

### 2024 presidential election

Main article: [2024 United States presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election")

Further information: [Donald Trump 2024 presidential campaign](/wiki/Donald_Trump_2024_presidential_campaign "Donald Trump 2024 presidential campaign"), [2024 Republican Party presidential primaries](/wiki/2024_Republican_Party_presidential_primaries "2024 Republican Party presidential primaries"), and [Second presidential transition of Donald Trump](/wiki/Second_presidential_transition_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidential transition of Donald Trump")

In November 2022, Trump announced his candidacy for the 2024 presidential election and created a fundraising account.[[374]](#cite_note-376)[[375]](#cite_note-377) In March 2023, the campaign began diverting ten percent of the donations to his [leadership PAC](/wiki/Leadership_PAC "Leadership PAC"). His campaign had paid $100 million towards his legal bills by March 2024.[[376]](#cite_note-378)[[377]](#cite_note-379)

During the campaign, Trump made increasingly violent and authoritarian statements.[[378]](#cite_note-380)
He said that he would weaponize the FBI and the Justice Department against his political opponents[[379]](#cite_note-381)[[380]](#cite_note-382) and use the military to target Democratic politicians and those not supporting his candidacy.[[381]](#cite_note-383)[[382]](#cite_note-Lerer_10152024-384) He used harsher and more dehumanizing anti-immigrant rhetoric than during his presidency.[[383]](#cite_note-385)
His rhetoric, calling his political opponents "the enemy", vermin, and fascists, has been described by some historians and scholars as authoritarian, [fascist](/wiki/Donald_Trump_and_fascism "Donald Trump and fascism"),[[384]](#cite_note-386)
and unlike anything a political candidate has ever said in American history.[[385]](#cite_note-NYMag_Never_Sounded_Like_This-387)[[382]](#cite_note-Lerer_10152024-384)[[386]](#cite_note-Applebaum18-388) [Age and health concerns](/wiki/Age_and_health_concerns_about_Donald_Trump "Age and health concerns about Donald Trump") also arose during the campaign, with several medical experts highlighting an increase in rambling, [tangential speech](/wiki/Tangential_speech "Tangential speech") and [behavioral disinhibition](/wiki/Disinhibition "Disinhibition").[[387]](#cite_note-389)

Trump mentioned "rigged election" and "election interference" earlier and more frequently than in the 2016 and 2020 campaigns and refused to commit to accepting the 2024 election results.[[388]](#cite_note-390)[[389]](#cite_note-NYTDoubts-391) On July 13, 2024, Trump was shot in the ear in [an assassination attempt](/wiki/Attempted_assassination_of_Donald_Trump_in_Pennsylvania "Attempted assassination of Donald Trump in Pennsylvania") at a campaign rally in [Butler Township, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Butler_Township,_Butler_County,_Pennsylvania "Butler Township, Butler County, Pennsylvania").[[390]](#cite_note-Browne-2024-392)[[391]](#cite_note-393)[[392]](#cite_note-394) Two days later, the [2024 Republican National Convention](/wiki/2024_Republican_National_Convention "2024 Republican National Convention") nominated him as their presidential candidate.[[393]](#cite_note-395) In September he was targeted but unharmed in [another assassination attempt](/wiki/Attempted_assassination_of_Donald_Trump_in_Florida "Attempted assassination of Donald Trump in Florida") in Florida.[[394]](#cite_note-396) Trump won the election in November 2024 with 312 electoral votes to incumbent vice president Kamala Harris's 226.[[395]](#cite_note-397) He also won the popular vote with 49.8% to Harris's 48.3%.[[396]](#cite_note-398) His victory in 2024 was in large part due to the [2021–2023 inflation surge](/wiki/2021%E2%80%932023_inflation_surge "2021–2023 inflation surge").[[397]](#cite_note-Global_Politics-399)[[398]](#cite_note-400) Several outlets described his reelection as an extraordinary comeback.[[399]](#cite_note-401)[[400]](#cite_note-402)

Trump's policy platform ([Agenda 47](/wiki/Agenda_47 "Agenda 47")) was vague, to limit criticism and maintain flexibility.[[401]](#cite_note-Bump-403) Trump occasionally disavowed knowledge of the [Project 2025](/wiki/Project_2025 "Project 2025") group, formed to institutionalize [Trumpism](/wiki/Trumpism "Trumpism"),[[402]](#cite_note-404) despite personnel overlap with his first administration.[[403]](#cite_note-CNN_review-405)[[404]](#cite_note-myths_and_facts-406) Trump chose several Project 2025 authors for his second administration,[[405]](#cite_note-407) and most of his early second term executive actions would "mirror or partially mirror" its proposals.[[406]](#cite_note-:8-408)

## Second presidency (2025–present)

Main article: [Second presidency of Donald Trump](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of the Donald Trump presidencies § Second presidency (2025–present)](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_Donald_Trump_presidencies#Second_presidency_(2025–present) "Timeline of the Donald Trump presidencies").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Donald_Trump_takes_the_oath_of_office_%282025%29_%28alternate%29.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_takes_the_oath_of_office_%282025%29_%28alternate%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_takes_the_oath_of_office_(2025)_(alternate).jpg)

Taking the [oath of office](/wiki/Oath_of_office_of_the_President_of_the_United_States "Oath of office of the President of the United States"), administered by [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States") [John Roberts](/wiki/John_Roberts "John Roberts"), January 20, 2025

Trump began his second term upon [his inauguration](/wiki/Second_inauguration_of_Donald_Trump "Second inauguration of Donald Trump") on January 20, 2025.[[407]](#cite_note-409) He became the [oldest to assume the presidency](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States_by_age "List of presidents of the United States by age"),[[408]](#cite_note-410) the first president with a felony conviction, and the second person to serve two nonconsecutive terms as president.[[409]](#cite_note-411) *The New York Times* stated his attempts to expand presidential power are a defining characteristic of his second term, and they emphasized his conflicts with judicial authority.[[410]](#cite_note-412)

### Early actions

See also: [First 100 days of the second Trump presidency](/wiki/First_100_days_of_the_second_Trump_presidency "First 100 days of the second Trump presidency")

More than a month before the 100-day mark of Trump's second term, he had [issued more executive orders](/wiki/List_of_executive_orders_in_the_second_Trump_presidency "List of executive orders in the second Trump presidency") than any other U.S. president's first 100 days.[[411]](#cite_note-413) As of mid-January 2026, his orders and actions had been challenged in over 550 lawsuits nationwide. Of these, plaintiffs had prevailed in 195 cases, the government had prevailed in 109 cases, there were split rulings in 16 cases, 228 cases were pending, and 25 cases were closed.[[412]](#cite_note-414) Both Republican and Democratic judicial appointees have found numerous constitutional and statutory flaws with Trump administration policies.[[413]](#cite_note-415)

Following legal setbacks, Trump increased his criticism of the judiciary and called for impeachment of federal judges who ruled against him.[[414]](#cite_note-416) By mid-July 2025, a *[Washington Post](/wiki/Washington_Post "Washington Post")* analysis found he defied judges and the courts in roughly one third of all cases against him, actions which were described by legal experts as unprecedented for any presidential administration.[[415]](#cite_note-417)

His administration asserted a constitutional right to ignore federal law in its justification of several actions such as in his refusal to enforce a [federal ban on TikTok](/wiki/Protecting_Americans_from_Foreign_Adversary_Controlled_Applications_Act "Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act"), with legal experts describing it as claiming a "constitutional power to immunize private parties to commit otherwise illegal acts with impunity".[[416]](#cite_note-418)[[417]](#cite_note-419)[[418]](#cite_note-420)

### Conflicts of interest (2025–present)

Further information: [Second presidency of Donald Trump § Ethics](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump#Ethics "Second presidency of Donald Trump"), and [Cryptocurrency in the second Trump presidency](/wiki/Cryptocurrency_in_the_second_Trump_presidency "Cryptocurrency in the second Trump presidency")

Trump's second presidency has been described as having fewer guardrails against conflicts of interest than his first,[[419]](#cite_note-Yourish_2025-421)[[420]](#cite_note-Schouten_2025-422) breaking with decades of ethical norms,[[421]](#cite_note-423) and raising substantial [corruption](/wiki/Corruption "Corruption") concerns.[[422]](#cite_note-Savage_05122025-424)[[423]](#cite_note-425) He maintained a publicly traded company in [Trump Media & Technology Group](/wiki/Trump_Media_%26_Technology_Group "Trump Media & Technology Group"), and diversified it into financial services.[[424]](#cite_note-426) He pursued new overseas real estate deals involving state-affiliated entities, and had several branding and licensing deals selling Trump-branded merchandise.[[419]](#cite_note-Yourish_2025-421) He profited from events held at his hotels and golf courses and did not place his assets in a blind trust, as previous presidents had done.[[425]](#cite_note-Schouten_1232025-427)

Trump launched, promoted, and personally benefited from [two cryptocurrency tokens](/wiki/The_Trump_Organization#Other_ventures_and_investments "The Trump Organization") ("[meme coins](/wiki/Meme_coin "Meme coin")"), [$Trump](/wiki/$Trump "$Trump") and $Melania.[[426]](#cite_note-Weissert_1222025-428)[[427]](#cite_note-429) He also directly benefited from his family's cryptocurrency company [World Liberty Financial](/wiki/World_Liberty_Financial "World Liberty Financial"), which engaged in an unprecedented mixing of private enterprise and government policy.[[428]](#cite_note-Lipton_04292025-430)

### Mass terminations of federal employees

Main article: [2025 United States federal mass layoffs](/wiki/2025_United_States_federal_mass_layoffs "2025 United States federal mass layoffs")

Trump implemented a hiring freeze across the federal government and ordered telework of federal employees to be discontinued within 30 days.[[429]](#cite_note-431)[[430]](#cite_note-upend-432) He ordered a review of many career civil service positions with the intention of [reclassifying them into at-will positions](/wiki/Policy/Career_appointment "Policy/Career appointment") without job protections.[[430]](#cite_note-upend-432)[[431]](#cite_note-433)[[432]](#cite_note-434) He initiated mass job terminations of federal employees,[[433]](#cite_note-Collinson-435) which were described by legal experts as unprecedented or in violation of federal law,[[434]](#cite_note-436) with the intent of replacing them with workers more aligned with his agenda.[[435]](#cite_note-437) By late February 2025, the administration had fired more than 30,000 people.[[436]](#cite_note-438) He ordered an end to [diversity, equity, and inclusion](/wiki/Diversity,_equity,_and_inclusion "Diversity, equity, and inclusion") (DEI) projects in the federal government and rescinded [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson")'s 1965 [Executive Order 11246](/wiki/Executive_Order_11246 "Executive Order 11246"), which had mandated that federal contractors take affirmative action to end racial discrimination.[[437]](#cite_note-439)[[438]](#cite_note-440)

Trump and [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk")'s [Department of Government Efficiency](/wiki/Department_of_Government_Efficiency "Department of Government Efficiency") largely dismantled several federal agencies including [USAID](/wiki/USAID "USAID") and the [Department of Education](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Education "United States Department of Education"), unilaterally fired several thousand staff, and reduced administrative functions to statutory minimums.[[439]](#cite_note-441)[[440]](#cite_note-442)[[441]](#cite_note-443)

### Targeting political opponents

Main article: [Targeting of political opponents and civil society under the second Trump administration](/wiki/Targeting_of_political_opponents_and_civil_society_under_the_second_Trump_administration "Targeting of political opponents and civil society under the second Trump administration")

During his second presidency, the Trump administration took a series of actions using the government to target political opponents and [civil society](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society"). He threatened, signed executive actions, and ordered investigations into his political opponents, critics, and organizations aligned with the Democratic Party.[[442]](#cite_note-444) He ended the post-[Watergate](/wiki/Watergate "Watergate") norm of [Justice Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") independence, weaponizing it and agencies across the federal government to target his political enemies.[[443]](#cite_note-445)[[444]](#cite_note-446) Trump's actions against civil society were described by hundreds of legal experts and political scientists as [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarian "Authoritarian"), contributing to [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding_in_the_United_States "Democratic backsliding in the United States"), and negatively impacting the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law").[[445]](#cite_note-447)[[446]](#cite_note-448)[[447]](#cite_note-449)

### Pardons and commutations (2025–present)

Further information: [List of people granted executive clemency in the second Trump presidency](/wiki/List_of_people_granted_executive_clemency_in_the_second_Trump_presidency "List of people granted executive clemency in the second Trump presidency")

Trump's pardons and grants of clemency favored political allies and loyalists,[[448]](#cite_note-450)[[449]](#cite_note-Thrush_May292025-451) and disproportionately pardoned "the powerful, famous, well-connected and wealthy" accused of white-collar crime.[[449]](#cite_note-Thrush_May292025-451) Trump [granted clemency to all January 6 rioters](/wiki/Pardon_of_January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack_defendants "Pardon of January 6 United States Capitol attack defendants") convicted or charged on his first day in office, including those who violently attacked police, by pardoning more than 1,500 and commuting the sentences of 14.[[450]](#cite_note-Reilly2025-452)[[451]](#cite_note-NPR2025-453)

### Domestic policy (2025–present)

Main articles: [Domestic policy of the second Trump administration](/wiki/Domestic_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration "Domestic policy of the second Trump administration"), [Economic policy of the second Trump administration](/wiki/Economic_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration "Economic policy of the second Trump administration"), [Education policy of the second Trump administration](/wiki/Education_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration "Education policy of the second Trump administration"), [Science policy of the second Trump administration](/wiki/Science_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration "Science policy of the second Trump administration"), and [Persecution of transgender people under the second Trump administration](/wiki/Persecution_of_transgender_people_under_the_second_Trump_administration "Persecution of transgender people under the second Trump administration")

Trump ordered agencies to stop enforcement of disfavored rules in an attempt at large-scale deregulation that legal experts described as illegal and contrary to decades of federal law.[[452]](#cite_note-454)[[453]](#cite_note-455) He sought greater government control over private businesses and shifted away from traditional conservative [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") orthodoxy,[[454]](#cite_note-456) engaging in [state capitalism](/wiki/State_capitalism "State capitalism") by taking direct government equity stakes in multiple U.S. corporations.[[455]](#cite_note-457)[[456]](#cite_note-Economist_08132025-458)[[457]](#cite_note-459)

#### Science

Trump [canceled and paused federal grants](/wiki/2025_United_States_federal_government_grant_pause "2025 United States federal government grant pause") and made large cuts to scientific research,[[458]](#cite_note-460) several of which were found by judges and the [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office "Government Accountability Office") as being illegal and unconstitutional.[[459]](#cite_note-461)[[460]](#cite_note-462) Trump and his administration's [Make America Healthy Again](/wiki/Make_America_Healthy_Again "Make America Healthy Again") agenda promoted [anti-science](/wiki/Anti-science "Anti-science") and [anti-vaccine activism](/wiki/Anti-vaccine_activism "Anti-vaccine activism"), resulting in a resurgence of whooping cough and measles.[[461]](#cite_note-463)[[462]](#cite_note-464)

#### National emergencies/health policy

Trump relied on declaring national emergencies to justify hundreds of actions and bypass congressional approval or regulatory review.[[463]](#cite_note-465) For instance, he declared a national energy emergency, allowing the suspension of environmental regulations, loosening the rules for fossil fuel extraction and limiting renewable energy projects.[[464]](#cite_note-466)[[465]](#cite_note-467) Trump withdrew the U.S. from the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") on climate change during his first presidency, [Biden](/wiki/Presidency_of_Joe_Biden "Presidency of Joe Biden") reentered the agreement in 2021, and Trump again withdrew from the agreement during his second presidency.[[466]](#cite_note-468)

#### Social

Trump attributed societal problems to [diversity, equity, and inclusion](/wiki/Diversity,_equity,_and_inclusion "Diversity, equity, and inclusion") (DEI) initiatives and [wokeness](/wiki/Woke#United_States "Woke").[[467]](#cite_note-Green250203-469) Equating diversity with incompetence,[[467]](#cite_note-Green250203-469) he reversed pro-diversity policies in the federal government.[[468]](#cite_note-470)[[469]](#cite_note-471) On DEI and antisemitism grounds, he threatened cultural institutions[[470]](#cite_note-472) and sixty universities,[[471]](#cite_note-WSJed-473) and forced law firms to capitulate to his political agenda.[[472]](#cite_note-APfirms-474)

Trump expanded the domestic use of the military and ordered [military deployments to several Democratic Party-led cities](/wiki/2025_deployment_of_federal_forces_in_the_United_States "2025 deployment of federal forces in the United States"), and threatened to expand his deployments further.[[473]](#cite_note-475)[[474]](#cite_note-476)

Through [a series of executive orders](/wiki/Persecution_of_transgender_people_under_the_second_Trump_administration#Executive_Orders "Persecution of transgender people under the second Trump administration") and other actions, Trump banned transgender people from the military;[[475]](#cite_note-477) restricted or defunded gender-affirming healthcare; opposed [inclusive language](/wiki/Inclusive_language "Inclusive language");[[476]](#cite_note-478) censored research and education materials;[[477]](#cite_note-479) targeted schools, universities, and cultural institutions accused of promoting what his government calls "gender ideology";[[478]](#cite_note-npr-ideology-480) barred transgender athletes from sports; and required U.S. passports to state transgender people's sex assigned at birth.[[479]](#cite_note-481)

#### One Big Beautiful Bill Act

In July 2025, Trump signed the [One Big Beautiful Bill Act](/wiki/One_Big_Beautiful_Bill_Act "One Big Beautiful Bill Act") into law. The bill made the temporary tax cuts of the 2017 [Tax Cuts and Jobs Act](/wiki/Tax_Cuts_and_Jobs_Act "Tax Cuts and Jobs Act") permanent and added additional tax deductions for a total of around $4.5 trillion, mostly benefiting the highest income brackets and costing people in the lowest income bracket $1,600 per year. It increased funding for national defense, deportations, the border wall, and Trump's proposed [Golden Dome](/wiki/Golden_Dome_(missile_defense_system) "Golden Dome (missile defense system)") missile defense system. It removed tax credits for renewable energy sources such as wind and solar power and for buyers of electric vehicles. The bill cut funding for [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") and [SNAP](/wiki/Supplemental_Nutrition_Assistance_Program "Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program") and added additional work requirements for eligibility and a $35 co-payment for some Medicaid services; the cuts and additional requirements will take effect after the 2026 general election. The bill was projected by the [Congressional Budget Office](/wiki/Congressional_Budget_Office "Congressional Budget Office") to increase the budget deficit by $3.4 trillion by 2034, cause 11.8 million people to lose Medicaid coverage, and eliminate SNAP benefits for three million people.[[480]](#cite_note-482)[[481]](#cite_note-483)

### Immigration (2025–present)

Main articles: [Immigration policy of the second Trump administration](/wiki/Immigration_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration "Immigration policy of the second Trump administration") and [Deportation in the second Trump administration](/wiki/Deportation_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Deportation in the second Trump administration")

Further information: [Mexico–United States border crisis § Second Trump administration (2025–present)](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_crisis#Second_Trump_administration_(2025–present) "Mexico–United States border crisis"), [Mexico–United States border wall § Second Trump administration (2025–present)](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border_wall#Second_Trump_administration_(2025–present) "Mexico–United States border wall"), and [Visa and deportation controversies in the second Trump administration](/wiki/Visa_and_deportation_controversies_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Visa and deportation controversies in the second Trump administration")

During Trump's first days in office, he instructed Border Patrol agents to summarily deport [illegal immigrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States "Illegal immigration to the United States") crossing the border and disabled the [CBP One](/wiki/CBP_One "CBP One") app that was being used to schedule border crossings. Trump resumed the [Remain in Mexico](/wiki/Remain_in_Mexico "Remain in Mexico") policy, designated drug cartels as terrorist groups, and ordered construction to be resumed on a border wall.[[482]](#cite_note-484)[[483]](#cite_note-Cbsjan28-485) He also revoked the parole status of immigrants who entered the U.S. under CBP One and ended [humanitarian parole for Cubans, Haitians, Nicaraguans](/wiki/Parole_for_Cubans,_Haitians,_Nicaraguans,_and_Venezuelans "Parole for Cubans, Haitians, Nicaraguans, and Venezuelans").[[483]](#cite_note-Cbsjan28-485) In March 2025, he used the [Alien Enemies Act](/wiki/Alien_Enemies_Act "Alien Enemies Act") of 1798 to [imprison illegal immigrants without trial](/wiki/March_2025_American_deportations_of_Venezuelans "March 2025 American deportations of Venezuelans") at the [Terrorism Confinement Center](/wiki/Terrorism_Confinement_Center "Terrorism Confinement Center") in El Salvador.[[484]](#cite_note-486) Trump targeted activists, legal immigrants, tourists, and students with visas who expressed criticism of his policies or pro-Palestinian advocacy.[[485]](#cite_note-487) Several [American citizens were detained and deported](/wiki/Detention_and_deportation_of_American_citizens_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Detention and deportation of American citizens in the second Trump administration").[[486]](#cite_note-488) His aggressive and hardline deportation campaign led to large nationwide protests and violent confrontations with migrants and protesters which increased in intensity following high-profile killings of U.S. citizens by federal agents during [Operation Metro Surge](/wiki/Operation_Metro_Surge "Operation Metro Surge") in January 2026.[[487]](#cite_note-489)[[488]](#cite_note-490)

### Foreign policy (2025–present)

Main article: [Foreign policy of the second Trump administration](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration "Foreign policy of the second Trump administration")

Further information: [Tariffs in the second Trump administration](/wiki/Tariffs_in_the_second_Trump_administration "Tariffs in the second Trump administration"), [China–United States trade war](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war "China–United States trade war"), [2025–2026 United States trade war with Canada and Mexico](/wiki/2025%E2%80%932026_United_States_trade_war_with_Canada_and_Mexico "2025–2026 United States trade war with Canada and Mexico"), [American expansionism under Donald Trump](/wiki/American_expansionism_under_Donald_Trump "American expansionism under Donald Trump"), and [2026 Iran war](/wiki/2026_Iran_war "2026 Iran war")

Trump's second term foreign policy was described as [expansionist](/wiki/American_expansionism_under_Donald_Trump "American expansionism under Donald Trump") and imperialist.[[489]](#cite_note-491)[[490]](#cite_note-492) His administration favored [hard power](/wiki/Hard_power "Hard power") to achieve its [America First](/wiki/America_First "America First") foreign policy goals,[[491]](#cite_note-493) and dismantled or withdrew support from domestic and international organizations dedicated to advancing American [soft power](/wiki/Soft_power "Soft power").[[492]](#cite_note-494)[[493]](#cite_note-495) Trump's relations with U.S. allies have been transactional and ranged from indifference to hostility, while he has sought friendlier relations with certain U.S. adversaries.[[494]](#cite_note-496)

Meeting with Zelenskyy, February 2025

In February 2025, Trump and Vice President Vance [berated Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy](/wiki/2025_Trump%E2%80%93Zelenskyy_Oval_Office_meeting "2025 Trump–Zelenskyy Oval Office meeting") in a highly contentious televised meeting. Media outlets described it as an unprecedented public confrontation between an American president and a foreign [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state").[[495]](#cite_note-Liptak_20250228-497)[[496]](#cite_note-498)

Trump has taken a pro-Israel stance and continued [support for Israel in the Gaza war](/wiki/United_States_support_for_Israel_in_the_Gaza_war "United States support for Israel in the Gaza war").[[497]](#cite_note-499) From March to May 2025, Trump [launched an extensive aerial campaign](/wiki/March%E2%80%93May_2025_United_States_attacks_in_Yemen "March–May 2025 United States attacks in Yemen") against [Houthi](/wiki/Houthis "Houthis") targets in Yemen—his first major military operation in the Middle East during his second term.[[498]](#cite_note-500) As part of the 2025 [Iran–Israel war](/wiki/Twelve-Day_War "Twelve-Day War"), Trump ordered the [bombing of Iranian nuclear sites](/wiki/2025_United_States_strikes_on_Iranian_nuclear_sites "2025 United States strikes on Iranian nuclear sites") in June.[[499]](#cite_note-501) His [plan for a Gaza ceasefire deal](/wiki/Gaza_peace_plan "Gaza peace plan") between Israel and [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") was signed in October 2025, leading to the creation of the [Board of Peace](/wiki/Board_of_Peace "Board of Peace").[[500]](#cite_note-502) In February 2026, Trump authorized [joint U.S./Israeli air strikes on Iran](/wiki/2026_Iran_war "2026 Iran war")[[501]](#cite_note-503) and sent aircraft carriers and other combat ships to the Middle East. Israeli aerial bombs killed Iranian supreme leader [Ali Khamenei](/wiki/Ali_Khamenei "Ali Khamenei") and other Iranian leaders; Iran retaliated with rocket attacks on neighboring countries.[[502]](#cite_note-504)

Trump's economic policies have been described as [protectionist](/wiki/Protectionism_in_the_United_States "Protectionism in the United States"),[[503]](#cite_note-505) with him imposing tariffs on most countries, including large tariffs on major trading partners China, Canada, and Mexico.[[504]](#cite_note-506) He started a global trade war, imposing tariffs at the highest level since the 1930 [Smoot–Hawley Tariff Act](/wiki/Smoot%E2%80%93Hawley_Tariff_Act "Smoot–Hawley Tariff Act") at the onset of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[505]](#cite_note-507)[[506]](#cite_note-508) In February 2026, the Supreme Court ruled the tariffs he imposed under the [International Emergency Economic Powers Act](/wiki/International_Emergency_Economic_Powers_Act "International Emergency Economic Powers Act") to be illegal.[[507]](#cite_note-509) Trump frequently threatened and enacted tariffs against treaty allies for opposing his political objectives.[[508]](#cite_note-510)[[509]](#cite_note-511)

In Latin America, Trump pursued legally controversial military strikes against [alleged drug boats](/wiki/United_States_strikes_on_alleged_drug_traffickers_during_Operation_Southern_Spear "United States strikes on alleged drug traffickers during Operation Southern Spear") in international waters,[[510]](#cite_note-512) and ordered [a large military buildup](/wiki/United_States_military_buildup_in_the_Caribbean_during_Operation_Southern_Spear "United States military buildup in the Caribbean during Operation Southern Spear").[[511]](#cite_note-513) In November 2025, Trump pardoned former Honduran president [Juan Orlando Hernández](/wiki/Juan_Orlando_Hern%C3%A1ndez "Juan Orlando Hernández"), who had been extradited to the U.S. in 2022 and sentenced to 45 years in prison for drug trafficking. In January 2026, the U.S. captured Venezuelan president [Nicolás Maduro](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Maduro "Nicolás Maduro") in [a military raid in Venezuela](/wiki/2026_United_States_strikes_in_Venezuela#Capture_of_Maduro "2026 United States strikes in Venezuela") and took him to New York, where he was charged with drug trafficking.[[512]](#cite_note-514)

In 2025, Trump [threatened the annexation of Greenland](/wiki/Greenland_crisis "Greenland crisis"), including by military force or the imposition of tariffs on European allies. In January 2026, he appeared to withdraw both threats, stating that he had reached "the framework of a future deal" with NATO.[[513]](#cite_note-515)

### Personnel (2025–present)

Main articles: [Political appointments of the second Trump administration](/wiki/Political_appointments_of_the_second_Trump_administration "Political appointments of the second Trump administration") and [Second cabinet of Donald Trump](/wiki/Second_cabinet_of_Donald_Trump "Second cabinet of Donald Trump")

In his second term, Trump selected [cabinet](/wiki/Second_cabinet_of_Donald_Trump "Second cabinet of Donald Trump") members with personal loyalty to him,[[514]](#cite_note-516)[[515]](#cite_note-focus-517) with the "focus on loyalty over subject-matter expertise".[[515]](#cite_note-focus-517) In February 2025, the White House stated that [Elon Musk](/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk") was a [special government employee](/wiki/Special_government_employee "Special government employee").[[516]](#cite_note-musk-518) Trump gave Musk's [Department of Government Efficiency](/wiki/Department_of_Government_Efficiency "Department of Government Efficiency") (DOGE) access to many federal government agencies.[[516]](#cite_note-musk-518) Musk's teams operated in eighteen departments and agencies in the administration's first month,[[517]](#cite_note-519) including in the Treasury Department's $5 trillion payment system,[[518]](#cite_note-520) the [Small Business Administration](/wiki/Small_Business_Administration "Small Business Administration"), the [Office of Personnel Management](/wiki/Office_of_Personnel_Management "Office of Personnel Management"), and the [General Services Administration](/wiki/General_Services_Administration "General Services Administration").[[519]](#cite_note-521)

## Political practice and rhetoric

Main articles: [Political positions of Donald Trump](/wiki/Political_positions_of_Donald_Trump "Political positions of Donald Trump") and [Rhetoric of Donald Trump](/wiki/Rhetoric_of_Donald_Trump "Rhetoric of Donald Trump")

Starting with his advocacy of [birther](/wiki/Birther "Birther") conspiracy theories in the 2010s, Trump helped bring once-fringe far-right ideas and organizations into the mainstream.[[520]](#cite_note-522)
The [alt-right](/wiki/Alt-right "Alt-right") movement coalesced around and supported his candidacy due in part to [opposition to multiculturalism](/wiki/Opposition_to_multiculturalism "Opposition to multiculturalism") and [immigration](/wiki/Opposition_to_immigration "Opposition to immigration").[[521]](#cite_note-523)[[522]](#cite_note-524)[[523]](#cite_note-525)
During his 2016 campaign, Trump's politics and rhetoric led to the formation of a [political movement](/wiki/Political_movement "Political movement") known as [Trumpism](/wiki/Trumpism "Trumpism"),[[524]](#cite_note-526) which has been [compared to a cult of personality](/wiki/Trumpism#Cult_of_personality "Trumpism").[[525]](#cite_note-527)
His political positions have been described as [populist](/wiki/Populist "Populist")[[526]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoss2024298-528)[[527]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUrbinati2019-529) or [right-wing populist](/wiki/Right-wing_populist "Right-wing populist"),[[528]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampaniConcepciónSolerSavín2022-530)[[529]](#cite_note-531) and favoring an expansion of presidential power under a maximalist interpretation of the [unitary executive theory](/wiki/Unitary_executive_theory "Unitary executive theory").[[530]](#cite_note-532)[[531]](#cite_note-533)

Many of his actions and rhetoric have been described as [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarian "Authoritarian") and contributing to [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding_in_the_United_States "Democratic backsliding in the United States"),[[532]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParkerTowler2019505;_513-534)[[533]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaufmanHaggard2019-535)[[534]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoss2024300"Donald_Trump_is_broadly_seen_as_posing_an_existential_threat_to_American_democracy."-536) as well as establishing an ["us versus them" narrative](/wiki/In-group_and_out-group "In-group and out-group").[[535]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoss2024299-537) Trump's rhetoric has been described as using [fearmongering](/wiki/Fearmongering "Fearmongering") and [demagogy](/wiki/Demagogy "Demagogy") which [intensified during his 2024 presidential campaign](/wiki/Donald_Trump_2024_presidential_campaign#Fearmongering "Donald Trump 2024 presidential campaign").[[536]](#cite_note-538) He appeals to [evangelical Christians](/wiki/Trumpism#Christian_Trumpism "Trumpism") and [Christian nationalists](/wiki/Christian_nationalism_in_the_United_States "Christian nationalism in the United States"),[[537]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerryWhiteheadGrubbs2021229Christian_nationalist_ideology_was_among_the_strongest_predictors_of_Americans_voting_for_Trump_in_2016_and_remained_a_strong_predictor_of_intent_to_vote_for_him_prior_to_the_2020_election-539)[[538]](#cite_note-540) and regularly vilifies his political opponents as "evil".[[539]](#cite_note-541)

### Racial and gender views

Main articles: [Racial views of Donald Trump](/wiki/Racial_views_of_Donald_Trump "Racial views of Donald Trump") and [Donald Trump sexual misconduct allegations](/wiki/Donald_Trump_sexual_misconduct_allegations "Donald Trump sexual misconduct allegations")

Many of Trump's comments and actions have been characterized as racist.[[540]](#cite_note-542) Several studies and surveys found that racist attitudes fueled Trump's political ascent and were more important than economic factors in determining the allegiance of Trump voters.[[541]](#cite_note-543) He explicitly and routinely disparages racial, religious, and ethnic minorities,[[542]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStephens-Dougan2021302-544) and scholars consistently find that racist and [Islamophobic](/wiki/Islamophobic "Islamophobic") attitudes regarding blacks, immigrants, and Muslims are strong indicators of support for Trump.[[543]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELajevardiOskooii2018-545)[[544]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerman202176-546)

He has been accused of racism[[545]](#cite_note-547) for running full-page newspaper advertisements and insisting that a group of five black and Latino teenagers were guilty of raping a white woman in the 1989 [Central Park jogger case](/wiki/Central_Park_jogger_case "Central Park jogger case"), even after they were cleared of suspicion in 2002.[[546]](#cite_note-548) Trump's comments on the 2017 [Unite the Right rally](/wiki/Unite_the_Right_rally "Unite the Right rally"), condemning "this egregious display of hatred, bigotry and violence on many sides" and stating that there were "very fine people on both sides", were criticized as implying a [moral equivalence](/wiki/Moral_equivalence "Moral equivalence") between the [white supremacist](/wiki/White_supremacist "White supremacist") demonstrators and the counter-protesters.[[547]](#cite_note-549) In a January 2018 discussion of immigration legislation, he reportedly referred to El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, and African nations as "shithole countries",[[548]](#cite_note-550) remarks condemned as racist.[[549]](#cite_note-551) In July 2019, a House of Representatives [resolution condemned Trump for racist remarks](/wiki/Racial_views_of_Donald_Trump#Democratic_congresswomen_should_"go_back"_to_their_countries "Racial views of Donald Trump") about four minority Democratic congresswomen.[[550]](#cite_note-552) His 2024 campaign made extensive use of [dehumanizing language](/wiki/Dehumanization "Dehumanization") and [racial stereotypes](/wiki/Racial_stereotypes "Racial stereotypes").[[551]](#cite_note-553)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/President_Trump_Visits_St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_%2849964153176%29.jpg/250px-President_Trump_Visits_St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_%2849964153176%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Trump_Visits_St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_(49964153176).jpg)

With a group of officials and advisors walking from the White House to St. John's Church, following the [forced removal of protesters at Lafayette Square](/wiki/Donald_Trump_photo_op_at_St._John%27s_Church#Clearing_Lafayette_Square_and_St._John's "Donald Trump photo op at St. John's Church")

In June 2020, during the [George Floyd protests](/wiki/George_Floyd_protests "George Floyd protests"), federal law-enforcement officials used tear gas and other crowd control tactics to remove a largely peaceful crowd of lawful protesters from [Lafayette Square](/wiki/Lafayette_Square,_Washington,_D.C. "Lafayette Square, Washington, D.C."), outside the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House").[[552]](#cite_note-wb-554)[[553]](#cite_note-bumpline-555) Trump then [posed with a Bible for a photo op](/wiki/Donald_Trump_photo_op_at_St._John%27s_Church "Donald Trump photo op at St. John's Church") at the nearby [St. John's Episcopal Church](/wiki/St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church,_Lafayette_Square "St. John's Episcopal Church, Lafayette Square"),[[552]](#cite_note-wb-554)[[554]](#cite_note-556)[[555]](#cite_note-557) with religious leaders condemning both the treatment of protesters and the photo opportunity itself.[[556]](#cite_note-558)

Trump has a history of belittling women when speaking to the media and on social media.[[557]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERotheCollins2019-559)[[558]](#cite_note-Shear-560) He made lewd comments, disparaged women's physical appearances, and referred to them using derogatory epithets.[[558]](#cite_note-Shear-560) In October 2016, a 2005 "[hot mic](/wiki/Hot_mic "Hot mic")" recording surfaced in which [he bragged](/wiki/Donald_Trump_Access_Hollywood_tape "Donald Trump Access Hollywood tape") about kissing and groping women without their consent, saying that, "when you're a star, they let you do it. You can do anything. ... Grab 'em by the [pussy](/wiki/Pussy#Female_genitalia "Pussy")."[[559]](#cite_note-561) He characterized the comments as "locker-room talk".[[560]](#cite_note-562)[[561]](#cite_note-563) The incident's widespread media exposure led to his first public apology, videotaped during his 2016 presidential campaign.[[562]](#cite_note-564) As of 2020, 26 women have publicly [accused him of sexual misconduct](/wiki/Donald_Trump_sexual_misconduct_allegations "Donald Trump sexual misconduct allegations"), including rape, kissing without consent, groping, looking under women's skirts, and walking in on naked teenage pageant contestants. He has denied the allegations.[[563]](#cite_note-no26-565)

### Link to violence and hate crimes

Further information: [Rhetoric of Donald Trump § Violence](/wiki/Rhetoric_of_Donald_Trump#Violence "Rhetoric of Donald Trump")

Trump has been identified as a key figure in increasing political violence in the U.S., both for and against him.[[564]](#cite_note-566)[[565]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENacosShapiroBloch-Elkon2020-567)[[566]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPiazzaVan_Doren2022-568) He is described as embracing extremism, [conspiracy theories](/wiki/List_of_conspiracy_theories_promoted_by_Donald_Trump "List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump") such as [QAnon](/wiki/QAnon "QAnon"), and far-right [militia movements](/wiki/American_militia_movement "American militia movement") to a greater extent than any modern American president.[[567]](#cite_note-569)[[568]](#cite_note-570)

Research suggests that Trump's rhetoric is associated with an increased incidence of hate crimes,[[569]](#cite_note-571)[[570]](#cite_note-572) and that he has an emboldening effect on expressing prejudicial attitudes due to his normalization of explicit racial rhetoric.[[571]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStephens-Dougan2021306-573) Researchers have also argued that Trump's "negative characterisations of, and false claims made about, those who became the targets" of the mob at the January 6 riot was a case of [stochastic terrorism](/wiki/Stochastic_terrorism "Stochastic terrorism").[[572]](#cite_note-574)[[573]](#cite_note-575) Numerous defendants investigated or prosecuted for violent acts and hate crimes cited his rhetoric in arguing that they were not culpable or should receive leniency.[[574]](#cite_note-576)[[575]](#cite_note-577) A nationwide review by [ABC News](/wiki/ABC_News_(United_States) "ABC News (United States)") in May 2020 identified at least 54 criminal cases, from August 2015 to April 2020, in which he was invoked in direct connection with violence or threats of violence mostly by white men and primarily against minorities.[[576]](#cite_note-578) Trump's refusal to condemn the white supremacist [Proud Boys](/wiki/Proud_Boys "Proud Boys") during a 2020 presidential debate[[577]](#cite_note-579) and his comment, "Proud Boys, stand back and stand by", were said to have led to increased recruitment for the pro-Trump group.[[578]](#cite_note-580) Counterterrorism researchers described his normalization and [revisionist history](/wiki/Historical_revisionism "Historical revisionism") of the January 6 Capitol attack, and [grant of clemency to all January 6 rioters](/wiki/Pardon_of_January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack_defendants "Pardon of January 6 United States Capitol attack defendants"), as encouraging future political violence.[[579]](#cite_note-581)[[580]](#cite_note-582)

### False or misleading statements and conspiracy theories

Main articles: [False or misleading statements by Donald Trump](/wiki/False_or_misleading_statements_by_Donald_Trump "False or misleading statements by Donald Trump") and [List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump](/wiki/List_of_conspiracy_theories_promoted_by_Donald_Trump "List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump")

[![Chart depicting false or misleading claims made by Trump](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/2017-_Donald_Trump_veracity_-_composite_graph.png/500px-2017-_Donald_Trump_veracity_-_composite_graph.png)](/wiki/File:2017-_Donald_Trump_veracity_-_composite_graph.png)

[Fact-checkers](/wiki/Fact-checkers "Fact-checkers") from *The Washington Post*,[[581]](#cite_note-database-583) the *Toronto Star*,[[582]](#cite_note-584) and CNN[[583]](#cite_note-585) compiled data on "false or misleading claims" (orange background) and "false claims" (violet foreground).

Trump frequently makes false statements in public remarks,[[584]](#cite_note-finnegan-586)[[125]](#cite_note-whoppers-125) to an extent that was unprecedented in American politics.[[584]](#cite_note-finnegan-586)[[585]](#cite_note-glasser-587)[[586]](#cite_note-Konnikova-588) His false and misleading statements were documented by [fact-checkers](/wiki/Fact-checker "Fact-checker"), including at *The Washington Post*, which tallied 30,573 false or misleading statements made by him during his first presidency,[[581]](#cite_note-database-583) increasing in frequency over time.[[587]](#cite_note-TermUntruth-589) His falsehoods are a distinctive part of his political identity[[585]](#cite_note-glasser-587) and have been described as [firehosing](/wiki/Firehosing "Firehosing").[[588]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKakutani201894–104-590)

During the first 100 days of his second term, Trump relied on false, misleading, and exaggerated claims to justify his executive actions and policies.[[589]](#cite_note-591) Throughout the first year of the term, he continued to make untrue statements as frequently as before but with less variety while increasing the frequency of repeating a "core set" of falsehoods. [[590]](#cite_note-592)

Some of Trump's falsehoods were inconsequential,[[591]](#cite_note-593)[[592]](#cite_note-594) while others had more far-reaching effects, such as his unproven promotion of antimalarial drugs as a treatment for COVID-19,[[593]](#cite_note-595)[[594]](#cite_note-596) contributing to a U.S. shortage of these drugs and [panic buying](/wiki/Panic_buying "Panic buying") in Africa and South Asia.[[595]](#cite_note-597)[[596]](#cite_note-598) His attacks on mail-in ballots and other election practices weakened public faith in the integrity of the 2020 presidential election,[[597]](#cite_note-599)[[598]](#cite_note-600) while his disinformation about the COVID-19 pandemic delayed and weakened the national response to it.[[599]](#cite_note-NYT_4_11_20-601)[[600]](#cite_note-USAT-Disinfo-602)[[601]](#cite_note-603) He habitually does not apologize for his falsehoods.[[602]](#cite_note-604) Until 2018, the media rarely referred to his falsehoods as lies, including when he repeated demonstrably false statements.[[603]](#cite_note-605)[[604]](#cite_note-DBauder-606)[[605]](#cite_note-607)

Since before his first presidency, Trump has [promoted numerous conspiracy theories](/wiki/List_of_conspiracy_theories_promoted_by_Donald_Trump "List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump"). In 2011, Trump became the leading proponent of the racist ["birther" conspiracy theory](/wiki/Barack_Obama_citizenship_conspiracy_theories "Barack Obama citizenship conspiracy theories") that Barack Obama, the first black U.S. president, was not born in the United States,[[606]](#cite_note-608) and he claimed credit for pressuring the government to publish Obama's birth certificate, which he considered fraudulent.[[607]](#cite_note-609) Trump often prefaced his conspiracies by claiming he heard them from someone else,[[608]](#cite_note-Haberman2016-610) and promoted conspiracies such as [climate change denial](/wiki/Climate_change_denial "Climate change denial"), [alleged Ukrainian interference in U.S. elections](/wiki/Conspiracy_theories_related_to_the_2019_Trump%E2%80%93Ukraine_scandal "Conspiracy theories related to the 2019 Trump–Ukraine scandal"), and [vaccines and autism](/wiki/Vaccines_and_autism "Vaccines and autism").[[608]](#cite_note-Haberman2016-610)[[609]](#cite_note-611)[[610]](#cite_note-612) After the 2020 presidential election, he promoted conspiracy theories for his defeat that were characterized as "[the big lie](/wiki/Big_lie_(2020_United_States_presidential_election) "Big lie (2020 United States presidential election)")".[[611]](#cite_note-613)[[612]](#cite_note-614)

### Relationship with news media

Main article: [Donald Trump's conflict with the news media](/wiki/Donald_Trump%27s_conflict_with_the_news_media "Donald Trump's conflict with the news media")

[![Chart depicting an increase of searches for "fake news" following Trump's 2016 campaign](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/20241116_%22Fake_news%22_%28topic%29_on_Google_Trends.svg/500px-20241116_%22Fake_news%22_%28topic%29_on_Google_Trends.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20241116_%22Fake_news%22_(topic)_on_Google_Trends.svg)

Following his 2016 election campaign, Trump's attacks on the media as "fake news" led to increased searches of the topic.

In the 2016 campaign, Trump benefited from a record amount of free media coverage,[[613]](#cite_note-Cillizza-160614-615) estimated at $2 billion.[[614]](#cite_note-616) As a candidate and as president, he frequently accused the press of bias, calling it the "fake news media" and "the [enemy of the people](/wiki/Enemy_of_the_people "Enemy of the people")".[[615]](#cite_note-617)

The first Trump presidency reduced formal press briefings from about one hundred in 2017 to two by 2019 and revoked the press passes of two White House reporters, which were restored by the courts.[[616]](#cite_note-618) By 2024, Trump repeatedly voiced support for [outlawing political dissent](/wiki/Criminalization_of_dissent "Criminalization of dissent") and criticism,[[617]](#cite_note-619) and said that reporters should be prosecuted for not divulging confidential sources and media companies should possibly lose their broadcast licenses for unfavorable coverage of him.[[618]](#cite_note-620)

In his second term, Trump's actions against the media were unprecedented in modern American history,[[619]](#cite_note-621) and historians described them as mirroring actions by authoritarian leaders to censor political opponents and negatively impacting the [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech") and [free press](/wiki/Free_press "Free press").[[620]](#cite_note-622)[[621]](#cite_note-623) The campaign to police speech drew comparisons to [cancel culture](/wiki/Cancel_culture "Cancel culture"), government [censorship](/wiki/Censorship "Censorship"), and [McCarthyism](/wiki/McCarthyism "McCarthyism").[[622]](#cite_note-624)[[623]](#cite_note-625)[[624]](#cite_note-626) The [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") launched investigations into media outlets accused of bias against him.[[625]](#cite_note-627) As a result of Trump's threats, media executives instructed journalists and their staff to [self-censor](/wiki/Self-censor "Self-censor") and reduce criticism of Trump.[[626]](#cite_note-628) Many have characterized Trump as causing a significant decline in [freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press"), including journalist advocacy groups[[627]](#cite_note-FerragamoCFR2025-629)[[628]](#cite_note-RSFUnitedStates2025-630)[[629]](#cite_note-CPJPressFreedomUS-631)[[630]](#cite_note-632) and academic sources.[[631]](#cite_note-633)[[632]](#cite_note-634)[[633]](#cite_note-635)

### Social media

Main articles: [Social media use by Donald Trump](/wiki/Social_media_use_by_Donald_Trump "Social media use by Donald Trump") and [Twitter use by Donald Trump](/wiki/Twitter_use_by_Donald_Trump "Twitter use by Donald Trump")

During Trump's first term, he often used Twitter to communicate directly with the public and sideline the press.[[634]](#cite_note-gone-636) After years of criticism for allowing Trump to post misinformation and falsehoods, Twitter began to tag some of his tweets with fact-checks in May 2020.[[635]](#cite_note-637) Twitter banned him after the January 6 attack.[[636]](#cite_note-638) He was banned from [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook"), [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram"), Twitter, and other platforms after the January 6 attack.[[637]](#cite_note-639) The loss of his social media presence diminished his ability to shape events[[638]](#cite_note-640)[[639]](#cite_note-641) and correlated with a dramatic decrease in the volume of misinformation on Twitter.[[640]](#cite_note-642) In February 2022, he launched social media platform [Truth Social](/wiki/Truth_Social "Truth Social") where he attracted a fraction of his Twitter following.[[641]](#cite_note-643) Twitter reinstated his account in November 2022.[[642]](#cite_note-644)[[643]](#cite_note-645) The two-year ban at [Meta Platforms](/wiki/Meta_Platforms "Meta Platforms") lapsed in January 2023, allowing him to return to Facebook and Instagram.[[644]](#cite_note-646)

## Assessments

### Public image

Main articles: [Public image of Donald Trump](/wiki/Public_image_of_Donald_Trump "Public image of Donald Trump") and [Opinion polling on the first Trump presidency](/wiki/Opinion_polling_on_the_first_Trump_presidency "Opinion polling on the first Trump presidency")

In Trump's first term, from 2017 to 2021, international approval ratings of U.S. leadership dropped from about 22 percent in a [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup,_Inc. "Gallup, Inc.") poll[[645]](#cite_note-647) of 134 countries to 16 percent—lower than China's [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") and Russia's [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin")—in a [Pew Research](/wiki/Pew_Research "Pew Research") poll[[646]](#cite_note-648) of 13 countries. In 2017, estimation of U.S. leadership declined most among allies.[[647]](#cite_note-649) Domestically, Trump had chiefly partisan support: 88 percent among Republicans and 7 percent among Democrats.[[648]](#cite_note-Jones-650) In a 2021 Gallup poll, he was the only president never to reach a 50 percent approval rating, and he was the first not to be named most admired in his first year in office.[[649]](#cite_note-651)

### Scholarly rankings

Further information: [Historical rankings of presidents of the United States](/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "Historical rankings of presidents of the United States")

After Trump's first term, [historians ranked Trump](/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#2021_C-SPAN "Historical rankings of presidents of the United States") as the fourth-worst president in [C-SPAN](/wiki/C-SPAN "C-SPAN")'s 2021 survey of presidential historians.[[650]](#cite_note-652) He rated lowest in the leadership characteristics categories for moral authority and administrative skills.[[651]](#cite_note-653)[[652]](#cite_note-654) The [Siena College Research Institute](/wiki/Siena_College_Research_Institute "Siena College Research Institute")'s 2022 survey [ranked him](/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#2022_Siena_College "Historical rankings of presidents of the United States") third-worst. He was ranked near the bottom in all categories except for luck, willingness to take risks, and party leadership, and ranked last in several categories.[[653]](#cite_note-scri_22-655) In 2018 and 2024, the [American Political Science Association](/wiki/American_Political_Science_Association "American Political Science Association") ranked him the worst president.[[654]](#cite_note-656)[[655]](#cite_note-657)

## Personal life

### Family

Main article: [Trump family](/wiki/Trump_family "Trump family")

In 1977, Trump married [Ivana Zelníčková](/wiki/Ivana_Zeln%C3%AD%C4%8Dkov%C3%A1 "Ivana Zelníčková").[[656]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlair2015300-658) They had three children: [Donald Jr.](/wiki/Donald_Jr. "Donald Jr.") (b. 1977), [Ivanka](/wiki/Ivanka_Trump "Ivanka Trump") (b. 1981), and [Eric](/wiki/Eric_Trump "Eric Trump") (b. 1984). The couple divorced in 1990, following his affair with model and actress [Marla Maples](/wiki/Marla_Maples "Marla Maples").[[657]](#cite_note-659) He and Maples married in 1993 and divorced in 1999. They have one daughter, [Tiffany](/wiki/Tiffany_Trump "Tiffany Trump") (b. 1993), whom Maples raised in California.[[658]](#cite_note-660) In 2005, he married Slovenian model [Melania Knauss](/wiki/Melania_Knauss "Melania Knauss").[[659]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKranishFisher2017266-661) They have one son, [Barron](/wiki/Barron_Trump "Barron Trump") (b. 2006).[[660]](#cite_note-662)

### Wealth

Main article: [Wealth of Donald Trump](/wiki/Wealth_of_Donald_Trump "Wealth of Donald Trump")

[![Ivana Trump and King Fahd shake hands, with Ronald Reagan standing next to them smiling](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Ivana_Trump_shakes_hands_with_Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia.jpg/250px-Ivana_Trump_shakes_hands_with_Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ivana_Trump_shakes_hands_with_Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia.jpg)

Trump (rightmost) and his wife Ivana at a 1985 state dinner for King [Fahd of Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia "Fahd of Saudi Arabia") with President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") and First Lady [Nancy Reagan](/wiki/Nancy_Reagan "Nancy Reagan")

Trump has said he began his career with "a small loan of a million dollars" from his father and that he had to pay it back with interest.[[661]](#cite_note-663) He borrowed at least $60 million from his father, largely did not repay the loans, and received another $413 million (2018 equivalent, adjusted for inflation) from his father's company.[[662]](#cite_note-664)[[36]](#cite_note-Tax_Schemes-36) Posing as a Trump Organization official named "[John Barron](/wiki/John_Barron_(pseudonym) "John Barron (pseudonym)")", Trump called journalist Jonathan Greenberg in 1984, trying to get a higher ranking on the [*Forbes* 400](/wiki/Forbes_400 "Forbes 400") list of wealthiest Americans.[[663]](#cite_note-665)

Trump self-reported his net worth over a wide range: from minus $900 million in 1990[[664]](#cite_note-666) to $10 billion in 2015.[[665]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnston202120-667) In 2015, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* estimated his net worth at $4.5 billion, based on interviews with more than 80 sources.[[666]](#cite_note-668) In 2026, the magazine estimated his net worth at $6.5 billion and ranked him the 645th wealthiest person in the world.[[667]](#cite_note-669)

### Relationship with Jeffrey Epstein

Main article: [Relationship of Donald Trump and Jeffrey Epstein](/wiki/Relationship_of_Donald_Trump_and_Jeffrey_Epstein "Relationship of Donald Trump and Jeffrey Epstein")

Trump had a 15-year friendship with convicted sex offender [Jeffrey Epstein](/wiki/Jeffrey_Epstein "Jeffrey Epstein"). Persons who knew them at the time said they frequently "hit on" and competed for women.[[668]](#cite_note-670) Media attention and public pressure mounted in 2025, when his administration did not release [files relating to Epstein](/wiki/Epstein_files "Epstein files"), despite Trump's promise to do so during the 2024 campaign.[[669]](#cite_note-671) Some of the files were released in December 2025, mostly heavily redacted.[[670]](#cite_note-672) Roughly three million additional pages released in 2026 included approximately 38,000 references to Trump, his wife, Mar-a-Lago, and other Trump-related terms. References to Trump included "salacious information" and uncorroborated statements about him from witnesses in transcripts made over several decades.[[671]](#cite_note-673)

### Health

Main article: [Age and health concerns about Donald Trump](/wiki/Age_and_health_concerns_about_Donald_Trump "Age and health concerns about Donald Trump")

Trump says he has never drunk alcohol, smoked cigarettes, or used drugs,[[672]](#cite_note-674)[[673]](#cite_note-675) and that he sleeps about four or five hours a night.[[674]](#cite_note-676)[[675]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlmondDu2020-677) He has called golfing his "primary form of exercise", but usually does not walk the course.[[676]](#cite_note-678) He considers exercise a waste of energy, believing the body is "like a battery, with a finite amount of energy" which is depleted by exercise.[[677]](#cite_note-679)[[678]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEO'DonnellRutherford1991133-680)

In 2015, his campaign released a letter from his longtime personal physician, [Harold Bornstein](/wiki/Harold_Bornstein "Harold Bornstein"), stating that he would "be the healthiest individual ever elected to the presidency".[[679]](#cite_note-dictation-681) In 2018, Bornstein said Trump had dictated the contents of the letter and that three of Trump's agents had seized his medical records in a February 2017 raid on Bornstein's office.[[679]](#cite_note-dictation-681)[[680]](#cite_note-682)

### Religion

Main article: [Donald Trump and religion](/wiki/Donald_Trump_and_religion "Donald Trump and religion")

Trump called himself a [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") and a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") in 2016.[[681]](#cite_note-683)[[682]](#cite_note-684) In 2020, he said he was a [nondenominational Christian](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christian "Nondenominational Christian").[[683]](#cite_note-685) However, many have questioned the depth of these religious affiliations. A survey during his first presidency (2017–2021) showed that 63 percent of Americans did not believe that he was religious, despite professing a Christian affiliation, and that only 44 percent believed he was a Christian.[[684]](#cite_note-686) Some of Trump's comments on the Bible or Christian practice have led critical observers to suggest that his knowledge of Christianity is superficial or erroneous, and few biographers have described him as deeply or even particularly religious.[[685]](#cite_note-LATimes17-687)[[686]](#cite_note-NPR16-688)

In his first term, Trump appointed his personal pastor[[687]](#cite_note-689) and spiritual advisor,[[688]](#cite_note-690) televangelist [Paula White-Cain](/wiki/Paula_White-Cain "Paula White-Cain"), to the White House [Office of Public Liaison](/wiki/Office_of_Public_Liaison "Office of Public Liaison").[[689]](#cite_note-691) During his second term, he appointed her as senior advisor of the newly created [White House Faith Office](/wiki/White_House_Faith_Office "White House Faith Office").[[690]](#cite_note-692)[[691]](#cite_note-693)
